questions.stem: A 3-month-old boy is evaluated for failure to thrive and lethargy. A CBC shows a macrocytic anemia that does not improve after several weeks of vitamin B12 and folate supplementation. His parents note a chalky, powdery residue in his diapers. Urinalysis reveals markedly elevated orotic acid, and serum ammonia is normal. Deficiency of which of the following enzymes is the most likely cause?

explanation.summary: Hereditary orotic aciduria results from a deficiency of UMP synthase, producing macrocytic anemia and orotic aciduria without hyperammonemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: UMP synthase (UMPS) converts orotic acid into UMP, the precursor of all pyrimidines. When it is deficient, orotic acid accumulates and is excreted (the powdery diaper residue), and impaired pyrimidine/DNA synthesis causes a macrocytic anemia that is unresponsive to B12 and folate. Normal ammonia distinguishes it from the urea-cycle cause of orotic aciduria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenosine deaminase deficiency disrupts purine catabolism and causes SCID with loss of B and T cells, not macrocytic anemia with orotic aciduria.
whyWrongByChoice.B: HGPRT deficiency (Lesch-Nyhan) impairs purine salvage, causing severe gout, self-mutilation, and neurologic findings, not orotic aciduria or anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ornithine transcarbamoylase deficiency also raises orotic acid but produces hyperammonemia and no anemia; this infant has normal ammonia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Xanthine oxidase acts in purine degradation to form uric acid; its inhibition lowers uric acid and is unrelated to pyrimidine synthesis or this presentation.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 4-month-old girl is diagnosed with hereditary orotic aciduria after presenting with a macrocytic anemia unresponsive to B12 and folate along with elevated urinary orotic acid. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term treatment?

explanation.summary: Uridine (or UMP) supplementation bypasses the UMPS block and restores pyrimidine production.
explanation.whyCorrect: In hereditary orotic aciduria the block is at UMP synthase. Administering uridine provides UMP downstream of the deficient enzyme, restoring synthesis of all other pyrimidines and correcting the DNA synthesis defect and anemia. This is a lifelong therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase to lower uric acid in gout; it has no role in pyrimidine synthesis or this anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The anemia is caused by an enzymatic block, not a vitamin deficiency, and is explicitly unresponsive to folate and B12.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A low-purine diet targets uric acid production in gout and does not address the pyrimidine synthesis defect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stem cell transplant is used for the immune failure of ADA-SCID, not for a pyrimidine biosynthetic enzyme deficiency correctable with uridine.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 3-day-old neonate develops lethargy, poor feeding, vomiting, and tachypnea. Laboratory studies show markedly elevated serum ammonia and increased urinary orotic acid, but hemoglobin and MCV are normal. Deficiency of which of the following enzymes best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: OTC deficiency causes orotic aciduria WITH hyperammonemia and no anemia, distinguishing it from hereditary orotic aciduria.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ornithine transcarbamoylase acts in the urea cycle just after CPSI. Its deficiency causes carbamoyl phosphate to accumulate and spill into the pyrimidine de novo pathway, raising orotic acid, while loss of urea cycle function produces hyperammonemia. Unlike hereditary orotic aciduria, there is no anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPSII is the cytoplasmic pyrimidine enzyme; its loss would decrease pyrimidine synthesis, not cause carbamoyl phosphate spillover, hyperammonemia, or elevated orotic acid.
whyWrongByChoice.C: UMPS deficiency causes orotic aciduria with macrocytic anemia and normal ammonia, whereas this infant has hyperammonemia and normal blood counts.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenosine deaminase deficiency causes SCID from disrupted purine catabolism, not hyperammonemia with orotic aciduria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ribonucleotide reductase makes deoxyribonucleotides and is unrelated to ammonia handling or orotic acid accumulation.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man undergoing radiation therapy for a rapidly shrinking tumor awakens with excruciating pain in his right great toe. The metatarsophalangeal joint of the hallux is swollen, red, warm, and exquisitely tender. His acute presentation is caused by overproduction of the end product of which of the following pathways?

explanation.summary: Massive cell turnover releases purines that are catabolized to uric acid, precipitating a gout flare.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rapid destruction of tumor cells liberates large amounts of purines that flux into the catabolic pathway, and the end product uric acid forms sodium urate crystals in the joint. Gout classically presents with rapid onset in the MTP joint of the hallux, exactly as described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: De novo pyrimidine synthesis yields UMP and other pyrimidines, not uric acid, and its excess does not cause gout.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gout results from purine breakdown (degradation), not from de novo synthesis of purines.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pyrimidine degradation products are highly water soluble and cause no known human disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Purine salvage recycles bases into nucleotides and does not overproduce uric acid.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with recurrent gout flares is started on allopurinol for long-term management. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Covalently inhibits xanthine oxidase to reduce uric acid production

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases renal excretion of uric acid

explanation.summary: Allopurinol is a purine analogue that inhibits xanthine oxidase, lowering uric acid synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Allopurinol is a well-tolerated free purine analogue that covalently binds the active site of xanthine oxidase, the enzyme that converts hypoxanthine and guanine into uric acid. Blocking it decreases uric acid production and is used for chronic gout control.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Inhibiting microtubule polymerization describes colchicine, which blunts crystal phagocytosis in acute flares.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increasing uric acid excretion (uricosuric action) describes probenecid, not allopurinol.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dihydrofolate reductase inhibition describes methotrexate, which limits the folate pool.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glutamine PRPP amidotransferase is inhibited by the 6-mercaptopurine metabolite TIMP, not by allopurinol.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with an acute, intensely painful, swollen first toe joint. Joint aspiration shows needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals. She is given colchicine for the acute attack. Which of the following best explains how colchicine relieves her symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It inhibits xanthine oxidase, lowering uric acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: It inhibits microtubule polymerization, preventing phagocytosis of urate crystals

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increases uric acid excretion in the urine

choices.label: D
choices.text: It dissolves sodium urate crystals directly

choices.label: E
choices.text: It inhibits PRPP synthetase to reduce purine production

explanation.summary: Colchicine inhibits microtubule polymerization, blocking neutrophil phagocytosis of urate crystals and the resulting inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Engulfment of sodium urate crystals by phagocytes drives cell damage and release of inflammatory mediators. Colchicine inhibits microtubule polymerization, preventing phagocytosis of the crystals and thereby attenuating the inflammatory response during an acute flare.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Xanthine oxidase inhibition is the mechanism of allopurinol, a preventive agent, not colchicine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increasing uric acid excretion is the uricosuric action of probenecid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Colchicine does not chemically dissolve existing urate crystals; it blocks the inflammatory response to them.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PRPP synthetase is not a target of colchicine, and its inhibition is not how acute gout inflammation is controlled.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy has intellectual disability, dystonia, and compulsive self-mutilation with bitten lips and fingers. Laboratory studies show markedly elevated uric acid, and he has had gout-like attacks. His disorder arises from a defect in an enzyme of which of the following processes?

explanation.summary: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome is an HGPRT deficiency in the purine salvage pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lesch-Nyhan results from deficiency of HGPRT, the major purine salvage enzyme. Without salvage, purines are shunted to uric acid and de novo synthesis loses feedback regulation, causing severe hyperuricemia/gout plus the characteristic neurologic impairment and self-mutilating behavior.

whyWrongByChoice.B: De novo pyrimidine synthesis defects (e.g., UMPS) cause orotic aciduria and macrocytic anemia, not hyperuricemia with self-mutilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A urea cycle defect such as OTC deficiency causes hyperammonemia, not this purine/uric acid syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pyrimidine salvage disorders are not associated with elevated uric acid or the neurobehavioral features of Lesch-Nyhan.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ribonucleotide reductase makes deoxyribonucleotides and is unrelated to this salvage-pathway disease.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant has had recurrent severe opportunistic infections since birth. Immunologic workup reveals a profound deficiency of both B and T lymphocytes. The responsible enzyme normally catalyzes which of the following reactions in nucleotide metabolism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deamination of AMP to inosine monophosphate during purine degradation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conversion of orotic acid to UMP

choices.label: C
choices.text: Addition of a methyl group to dUMP

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduction of the 2' carbon of ribose

explanation.summary: ADA deficiency causes SCID; adenosine deaminase normally deaminates AMP/adenosine to IMP for degradation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenosine deaminase deficiency causes a form of SCID (about 10-20% of cases) with loss of B and T cells. AMP is unique in that it must first be deaminated by ADA to form IMP before it can enter the common purine degradation pathway; loss of ADA disrupts nucleotide recycling and is toxic to lymphocytes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Conversion of orotic acid to UMP is done by UMP synthase; its deficiency causes hereditary orotic aciduria, not SCID.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methylating dUMP is the thymidylate synthase reaction, unrelated to SCID.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reducing the 2' carbon of ribose is the ribonucleotide reductase reaction, not the enzyme deficient in ADA-SCID.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PRPP formation is catalyzed by PRPP synthetase and is not the immunodeficiency-causing enzyme here.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: In the de novo purine synthesis pathway, one enzyme constitutes the committed, most heavily regulated step: it is activated by PRPP and inhibited by the downstream products AMP, GMP, and IMP. Which of the following is this enzyme?

explanation.summary: Glutamine PRPP amidotransferase is the committed, regulated step of de novo purine synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: This enzyme catalyzes the transfer of a nitrogen from glutamine onto carbon 1 of PRPP and is the committed step. It is feed-forward activated by PRPP and feedback inhibited by the end products AMP, GMP, and IMP, matching the description.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PRPP synthetase makes PRPP but is explicitly NOT the committed step, since PRPP has many fates beyond purine synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CPSII is the regulated step of pyrimidine, not purine, de novo synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Xanthine oxidase functions in purine degradation to uric acid, not in de novo synthesis regulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HGPRT is a purine salvage enzyme, not the committed de novo synthesis step.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A rapidly dividing cell must generate deoxyribonucleotide substrates for DNA replication. Which of the following enzymes accomplishes this by reducing the 2' carbon of the ribose sugar, using reducing power ultimately supplied by NADPH via thioredoxin?

explanation.summary: Ribonucleotide reductase converts ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides by reducing the ribose 2' carbon.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ribonucleotide reductase removes the 2' hydroxyl of the ribose sugar to produce the deoxyribose needed for DNA. The oxidation-reduction is powered by reduced thioredoxin, which is regenerated by thioredoxin reductase using NADPH from the pentose phosphate pathway. All nucleotides pass through RR except dTMP, which comes from dUMP.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thymidylate synthase methylates dUMP to form dTMP; it does not reduce the ribose 2' carbon.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dihydrofolate reductase regenerates FH4 from DHF and does not act on the sugar.
whyWrongByChoice.D: UMP synthase converts orotic acid to UMP in pyrimidine synthesis, not ribose reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PRPP synthetase adds pyrophosphate to ribose 5-phosphate and does not reduce the 2' carbon.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A patient with severe dietary folate deficiency is evaluated for impaired nucleotide synthesis. Because folate one-carbon donation is required for the synthesis of certain nucleotides but not others, production of which of the following nucleotides remains essentially unaffected?

explanation.summary: Folate (and B12) are required for dTMP, GMP, and AMP but not for CMP or UMP.
explanation.whyCorrect: The synthetic cascades show one-carbon folate donations are needed to make dTMP, GMP, and AMP, whereas UMP (and CMP) synthesis does not require folate. Thus in folate deficiency, UMP production is essentially unaltered even as the folate-dependent nucleotides fall.

whyWrongByChoice.A: dTMP synthesis by thymidylate synthase directly consumes a folate one-carbon unit, so it is suppressed in folate deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AMP is derived from IMP, whose purine ring requires folate one-carbon donations, so its production falls.
whyWrongByChoice.C: GMP is likewise derived from folate-dependent IMP synthesis and is decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IMP formation requires multiple folate-dependent one-carbon donations, so it is impaired in folate deficiency.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A patient with leukemia is treated with 6-mercaptopurine, an inert purine analogue prodrug. When it is salvaged by HGPRT it is converted to thioinosine monophosphate (TIMP), which mediates the drug's antineoplastic effect. TIMP acts by inhibiting which of the following enzymes?

explanation.summary: The 6-mercaptopurine metabolite TIMP potently inhibits glutamine PRPP amidotransferase, blocking de novo purine synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: When 6-mercaptopurine is salvaged by HGPRT it forms TIMP, a potent inhibitor of glutamine PRPP amidotransferase and other purine de novo enzymes. This deprives rapidly growing cancer cells of the purine substrates needed for DNA synthesis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Xanthine oxidase is inhibited by allopurinol, and it metabolizes 6-MP to 6-thiouric acid rather than being a TIMP target.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thymidylate synthase is inhibited by the 5-fluorouracil metabolite FdUMP, not by TIMP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dihydrofolate reductase is the target of methotrexate, not TIMP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ribonucleotide reductase is not the enzyme inhibited by the 6-mercaptopurine metabolite TIMP.

questions.topicName: Nitrogenous Base Metabolism

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the dermatologist because he develops severe blistering sunburns after only a few minutes of sun exposure. Examination shows extensive freckling and several actinic keratoses on sun-exposed skin, and he has already had one squamous cell carcinoma removed. Which DNA repair pathway is most likely deficient in this child?

explanation.summary: Xeroderma pigmentosum results from defective nucleotide excision repair, leaving UV-induced pyrimidine dimers unrepaired.
explanation.whyCorrect: UV light produces helix-distorting pyrimidine dimers that are corrected in humans by nucleotide excision repair (NER). Deficient NER activity causes xeroderma pigmentosum, marked by extreme photosensitivity, severe sunburn from brief exposure, and a greatly increased risk of skin cancer from accumulated unrepaired UV damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Base excision repair corrects single altered bases such as uracil or oxidized bases, not the bulky pyrimidine dimers caused by UV light.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mismatch repair corrects base-base mismatches from replication errors; its deficiency causes Lynch syndrome, not photosensitivity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Homologous recombination repairs double-strand breaks; its loss (e.g., BRCA mutation) predisposes to breast and ovarian cancer rather than xeroderma pigmentosum.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nonhomologous end joining repairs double-strand breaks; its defects cause SCID or ataxia-telangiectasia, not the photosensitive skin-cancer phenotype described.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A researcher irradiates cultured skin cells with ultraviolet light and then examines the DNA for structural changes. Which of the following lesions is most characteristic of this exposure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Covalent bonding between adjacent pyrimidines on the same strand

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of a purine base leaving an abasic site

choices.label: C
choices.text: Addition of a methyl group to the O6 position of guanine

choices.label: D
choices.text: A double-strand break severing the helix

explanation.summary: UV light causes neighboring pyrimidines to form abnormal covalent bonds, creating pyrimidine (thymine) dimers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Within a DNA strand, adjacent pyrimidine bases absorb UV light and form abnormal covalent bonds called pyrimidine dimers (mainly cyclobutane thymine dimers). Because the neighboring pyrimidines bond to each other rather than to their complementary bases, the helix is distorted and kinked, interfering with transcription and replication.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Depurination, which produces an abasic site, is an endogenous hydrolytic lesion repaired by base excision repair, not a UV-specific injury.
whyWrongByChoice.C: O6-methylguanine results from methylation/alkylation, an endogenous chemical modification, not from UV light.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Double-strand breaks are characteristic of ionizing radiation such as X-rays, not UV light.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Deamination of cytosine to uracil is a spontaneous endogenous event repaired by base excision repair, unrelated to UV exposure.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman is diagnosed with colorectal cancer. Her family history is notable for colorectal and endometrial cancers occurring in multiple relatives before age 50. Genetic testing reveals a germline mutation in MSH2. A defect in which of the following processes best explains her cancer predisposition?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Correction of base mismatches introduced during replication

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rejoining of double-strand breaks by homologous recombination

explanation.summary: Lynch syndrome arises from germline mutation in a mismatch repair gene, impairing correction of replication errors.
explanation.whyCorrect: MSH2 is one of the four mismatch repair (MMR) genes (with MLH1, MSH6, and PMS2). A germline mutation causes Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer), in which uncorrected replication mismatches accumulate. Affected individuals have markedly increased, often early-onset, colorectal and endometrial cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Direct repair (e.g., MGMT) removes methyl groups; it is not the MMR pathway affected by MSH2 mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pyrimidine dimer repair is performed by nucleotide excision repair, whose loss causes xeroderma pigmentosum, not Lynch syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Homologous recombination repairs double-strand breaks; BRCA (not MSH2) defects impair it and predispose to breast and ovarian cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Excision of oxidized single bases is base excision repair, a different pathway from the mismatch repair encoded by MSH2.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: During replication, a uracil residue is mistakenly incorporated into a strand of DNA. In the base excision repair pathway that corrects this error, which enzyme performs the first step by recognizing the abnormal base and removing it to create an apurinic/apyrimidinic site?

explanation.summary: Base excision repair begins when a DNA glycosylase excises the damaged base to form an AP site.
explanation.whyCorrect: To initiate BER, a DNA glycosylase recognizes a damaged or incorrect nucleotide (such as uracil) and removes its base, creating an AP (apurinic/apyrimidinic) site. An endonuclease then cleaves the 5' phosphodiester bond and a lyase cleaves the 3' bond, after which DNA polymerase fills the gap and ligase seals the nicks.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DNA ligase acts at the end of the pathway, sealing the nicks after the gap is filled, not in recognizing or removing the base.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA polymerase fills the single-nucleotide gap after the AP site is processed; it does not create the AP site.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Photolyase breaks pyrimidine dimers via photoreactivation and is absent in humans; it plays no role in base excision repair.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Helicase unwinds DNA (used in nucleotide excision repair to remove the segment) and does not excise the damaged base in BER.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A cell sustains spontaneous methylation producing O6-methylguanine. Humans correct this lesion using a direct repair mechanism. Which of the following best describes how this repair is accomplished?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The enzyme MGMT removes the anomalous methyl group without breaking the phosphodiester backbone

choices.label: B
choices.text: A stretch of nucleotides containing the lesion is excised and resynthesized

choices.label: C
choices.text: A homologous chromosome is used as a template to restore the sequence

choices.label: D
choices.text: Photolyase uses light energy to reverse the modification

choices.label: E
choices.text: A glycosylase removes the base to form an AP site

explanation.summary: Direct repair by O6-methylguanine methyltransferase (MGMT) reverses methylation without cutting the DNA backbone.
explanation.whyCorrect: Direct repair corrects damaged bases without severing the phosphodiester bonds that hold nucleotides together. Humans use the enzyme O6-methylguanine methyltransferase (MGMT) to detect and remove anomalous methyl groups, directly reversing the lesion.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Excising and resynthesizing a stretch of nucleotides describes nucleotide excision repair, which does break the backbone, not direct repair.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Template-directed repair using a homologous chromosome is homologous recombination, used for double-strand breaks.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Photolyase performs photoreactivation of pyrimidine dimers but is not present in humans and does not address methylation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycosylase-mediated base removal to form an AP site is the first step of base excision repair, which severs the backbone, unlike direct repair.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman is found to carry an inherited BRCA1 mutation that impairs the machinery normally used to accurately repair double-strand breaks using a homologous chromosome as a template. For which of the following is she at greatest increased risk later in life?

explanation.summary: BRCA1/BRCA2 support homologous recombination; their loss markedly raises breast and ovarian cancer risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: BRCA1 and BRCA2 play crucial roles in homologous recombination repair of double-strand breaks (BRCA2 regulates RAD51). Mutations impair accurate repair and genomic stability, significantly increasing the risk of breast and ovarian cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Xeroderma pigmentosum results from defective nucleotide excision repair, not from BRCA-associated homologous recombination defects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lynch syndrome is caused by mismatch repair gene mutations, not BRCA mutations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lung cancer is linked to tobacco-smoke carcinogens and receptor gene polymorphisms, not to inherited BRCA-mediated homologous recombination defects.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ataxia-telangiectasia is associated with ATM and defective double-strand break signaling/nonhomologous end joining, not with BRCA-driven homologous recombination.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: Of the DNA repair mechanisms, which is most likely to introduce a mutation into a cell's genome because it can join any two severed strands without requiring homology?

explanation.summary: Nonhomologous end joining is error-prone because it fuses DNA ends without a homologous template.
explanation.whyCorrect: NHEJ uses a ligase complex to directly fuse the ends of two DNA fragments. Because it connects any two severed strands it can find and does not require homology, it is error-prone and can introduce mutations; when breaks occur on separate chromosomes it can cause translocations.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Homologous recombination uses a homologous chromosome as a template, so it reconnects strands without loss of sequence and is not error-prone.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Base excision repair faithfully removes and replaces a single damaged base and is not a major source of mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nucleotide excision repair accurately removes and replaces a lesion-containing oligonucleotide using the intact strand as a template.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MGMT direct repair simply removes a methyl group without altering the sequence and does not introduce mutations.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A patient with epithelial ovarian cancer is treated with cisplatin. At the molecular level, which of the following best describes how this platinum-based drug damages tumor DNA to produce its antitumor effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It forms covalent adducts at the N7 position of guanine that evolve into intrastrand and interstrand crosslinks

choices.label: B
choices.text: It generates cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers between adjacent thymines

choices.label: C
choices.text: It deaminates cytosine residues to uracil

choices.label: D
choices.text: It directly cleaves both strands to create double-strand breaks

choices.label: E
choices.text: It inserts uracil into DNA during replication

explanation.summary: Platinum drugs covalently bind N7 of guanine, forming DNA crosslinks that drive their antitumor activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cisplatin and carboplatin interact with DNA to form mono-adducts, mostly covalently at the N7 of guanine. This mono-adduct then evolves into intrastrand crosslinks (the most abundant platinum adducts) or interstrand crosslinks, and the interstrand crosslinks are responsible for the antitumor activity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers are produced by UV light, not by platinum chemotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Deamination of cytosine to uracil is a spontaneous endogenous lesion, unrelated to platinum drug chemistry.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Direct double-strand cleavage is characteristic of ionizing radiation, not of platinum adduct formation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Uracil incorporation during replication is a replication/hydrolysis-related lesion repaired by base excision repair, not a platinum drug mechanism.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A cell's mitochondrial electron transport chain and a Fenton reaction generate a molecule with an unpaired electron that oxidizes DNA bases and can sever phosphodiester bonds. Which of the following is this reactive species?

explanation.summary: Hydroxyl radicals are reactive oxygen species with unpaired electrons that damage DNA in multiple ways.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) arise from endogenous sources such as the mitochondrial electron transport chain and Fenton reactions. The hydroxyl radical is a ROS produced from the reduction of hydrogen peroxide; because ROS have unpaired electrons, they are highly reactive and damage DNA by altering nucleotides, crosslinking strands, or severing phosphodiester bonds to cause strand breaks.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Molecular oxygen becomes reactive only after gaining an extra electron (forming superoxide); stable O2 itself is not the damaging radical described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A thymine dimer is a UV-induced DNA lesion, not a reactive oxygen species.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Uracil is a base that can be mistakenly incorporated into DNA; it is a lesion, not a reactive radical.
whyWrongByChoice.E: O6-methylguanine is a methylated base produced by alkylation, not an oxygen radical.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child presents with progressive gait ataxia, choreic movements, and dilated blood vessels visible in the conjunctivae. Workup reveals a mutation affecting a gene that, through the MRN complex, is activated in response to double-strand breaks and coordinates their repair. Which gene is most likely mutated?

explanation.summary: Ataxia-telangiectasia results from ATM mutation, impairing the double-strand break response downstream of the MRN complex.
explanation.whyCorrect: The proteins MRE11, RAD50, and NBS1 form the MRN complex in response to DNA damage and then activate ATM, the gene responsible for ataxia-telangiectasia. Affected children develop ataxia, chorea, myoclonus, and neuropathy usually before age 5, along with telangiectasias in the skin and eyes; NHEJ/double-strand break repair defects underlie this disorder.

whyWrongByChoice.B: MLH1 is a mismatch repair gene; its mutation causes Lynch syndrome, not ataxia-telangiectasia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: XPA is a nucleotide excision repair gene; its loss causes xeroderma pigmentosum with photosensitivity, not ataxia and telangiectasias.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BRCA2 regulates RAD51 in homologous recombination; mutations predispose to breast and ovarian cancer, not the neurologic phenotype described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MGMT performs direct repair of methylated guanine and is not activated by the MRN complex or linked to ataxia-telangiectasia.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: Nucleotide excision repair is especially important in humans for correcting UV-induced pyrimidine dimers. Which of the following best explains why humans rely so heavily on this pathway for these lesions?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Humans cannot perform base excision repair

choices.label: C
choices.text: Humans have no DNA polymerase with proofreading capability

choices.label: D
choices.text: Humans repair pyrimidine dimers only by nonhomologous end joining

choices.label: E
choices.text: Humans use MGMT to reverse pyrimidine dimers directly

explanation.summary: Because humans lack photolyase, nucleotide excision repair is the key mechanism for repairing pyrimidine dimers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Photolyase is an enzyme that breaks pyrimidine dimers through photoreactivation using light energy, but bacteria, fungi, plants, and some animals have it while humans do not. Because humans lack photolyase, nucleotide excision repair (which occurs primarily in G1) becomes the important mechanism for repairing UV-induced pyrimidine dimers.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Humans do perform base excision repair; it simply targets single altered bases rather than bulky pyrimidine dimers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Human DNA polymerases do have proofreading ability; this is unrelated to reliance on NER for dimers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nonhomologous end joining repairs double-strand breaks, not pyrimidine dimers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MGMT reverses O6-methylguanine, not pyrimidine dimers, so it cannot explain reliance on NER.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: An oncology team notes that both cigarette smoke and platinum chemotherapy damage DNA. Which statement most accurately reflects a mechanism described for tobacco smoke as a source of DNA damage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It induces aldehyde-driven DNA adducts and also reduces DNA repair

choices.label: B
choices.text: It reverses methylation of guanine residues

choices.label: C
choices.text: It repairs double-strand breaks by homologous recombination

choices.label: E
choices.text: It protects the lung genome from carcinogens

explanation.summary: Tobacco smoke forms aldehyde-driven DNA adducts and simultaneously impairs DNA repair, compounding damage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tobacco smoke contains at least 70 carcinogens and induces acrolein- and crotonaldehyde-DNA adducts in the lungs, with aldehydes being the major driving force of the damage. Importantly, in addition to causing damage, tobacco smoke also reduces DNA repair, so damage that could otherwise be repaired is left uncorrected.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Reversing methylation of guanine is the function of MGMT direct repair, not an action of tobacco smoke.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Homologous recombination is a cellular repair pathway; tobacco smoke damages DNA rather than repairing double-strand breaks.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pyrimidine dimers are caused by UV light; tobacco smoke acts mainly through aldehyde-DNA adducts, not exclusively dimers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tobacco smoke damages the lung genome and reduces repair; it does not protect against carcinogens.

questions.topicName: DNA Damage and DNA Repair Mechanism

questions.stem: A first-year medical student is studying the components of a DNA nucleotide. She learns that each nucleotide consists of a phosphate group, a nitrogenous base, and a pentose sugar. Which feature of the pentose sugar distinguishes a DNA nucleotide from an RNA nucleotide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hydroxyl group on the 3' carbon

choices.label: B
choices.text: A single hydrogen on the 2' carbon instead of a hydroxyl group

choices.label: C
choices.text: An additional phosphate on the 5' carbon

choices.label: D
choices.text: A methyl group replacing the base

choices.label: E
choices.text: A six-membered ring rather than a five-membered ring

explanation.summary: DNA uses deoxyribose, which lacks the 2' hydroxyl found in RNA's ribose.
explanation.whyCorrect: DNA nucleotides have a single hydrogen attached to the 2' carbon of the pentose sugar instead of a hydroxyl group, which is why the sugar is called deoxyribose. RNA nucleotides carry a hydroxyl at the 2' position on ribose.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both DNA and RNA sugars have a 3' hydroxyl; it is the phosphodiester linkage site, not the DNA/RNA distinction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A phosphate group is part of every nucleotide and does not distinguish deoxyribose from ribose.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The base is attached to the sugar in both DNA and RNA and is not replaced by a methyl group.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both ribose and deoxyribose are five-membered (pentose) sugars.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A researcher determines that a segment of double-stranded DNA is unusually resistant to strand separation, requiring a high melting temperature. Sequencing shows the region is rich in guanine-cytosine base pairs. Which molecular property best explains the elevated melting temperature?

choices.label: A
choices.text: G-C pairs are joined by three hydrogen bonds versus two for A-T pairs

choices.label: B
choices.text: G-C pairs form covalent phosphodiester bonds between strands

choices.label: C
choices.text: Purines pair only with other purines in G-C-rich regions

choices.label: D
choices.text: G-C pairs widen the diameter of the double helix

choices.label: E
choices.text: G-C pairs eliminate the antiparallel orientation of the strands

explanation.summary: G-C base pairs are held by three hydrogen bonds, making G-C-rich DNA more tightly bound.
explanation.whyCorrect: Guanine-cytosine pairs form three hydrogen bonds while adenine-thymine pairs form only two. More hydrogen bonds create stronger adhesion between the strands, so more thermal energy (higher melting temperature) is needed to separate G-C-rich DNA.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Phosphodiester bonds are covalent linkages within a single strand's backbone, not bonds between the two strands.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Base pairing is always purine-to-pyrimidine (A-T, G-C); purines do not pair with purines.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydrogen bond number affects binding strength, not the diameter of the helix.
whyWrongByChoice.E: All double-stranded DNA is antiparallel regardless of G-C content.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: While reviewing the structure of a DNA strand, a student notes that each strand has a defined directionality described as a 5' end and a 3' end. Which type of bond is responsible for creating this directional backbone by linking one nucleotide to the next?

explanation.summary: Phosphodiester bonds covalently link nucleotides and establish 5' and 3' ends.
explanation.whyCorrect: A covalent phosphodiester bond links the phosphate group of one nucleotide to the hydroxyl group of the sugar of the previous nucleotide. This repeating linkage forms the backbone and creates the distinct 5' and 3' ends of each polynucleotide strand.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hydrogen bonds hold the two complementary strands together, not the backbone within a strand.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ionic bonds describe attractions such as histone-DNA binding, not the covalent backbone linkage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Disulfide bonds form between cysteine residues in proteins, not in the DNA backbone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Van der Waals forces are weak transient interactions and do not form the covalent backbone.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A textbook figure shows two complementary DNA strands running in opposite directions so that the 3' end of one strand aligns with the 5' end of the other. This antiparallel arrangement is required for which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proper hydrogen bonding between complementary bases

choices.label: B
choices.text: Formation of covalent bonds between the two strands

choices.label: D
choices.text: Generation of a left-handed helix as the natural configuration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Neutralization of the negative charge on the phosphate backbone

explanation.summary: Antiparallel orientation allows complementary bases to align and hydrogen bond correctly.
explanation.whyCorrect: Complementary strands are oriented antiparallel, with the 3' end of one strand pairing with the 5' end of the complementary strand. This geometry is what permits the bases to align and form the hydrogen bonds that hold the double helix together.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds, not covalent bonds.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The sugar identity (deoxyribose) is fixed during nucleotide synthesis and is unrelated to strand orientation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The natural configuration of DNA is a right-handed helix, not left-handed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The exposed phosphates give DNA an overall negative charge; antiparallel orientation does not neutralize it.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A molecular biology lecture emphasizes that the naturally occurring DNA helix is right-handed, with about 10 base pairs per complete turn and roughly 0.34 nm between adjacent base pairs. The helix also displays a major groove and a minor groove. What is the primary functional importance of these grooves?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They store extra nucleotides for repair

choices.label: B
choices.text: They provide surfaces where proteins bind to carry out processes such as transcription and replication

choices.label: C
choices.text: They neutralize the negative charge of the backbone

choices.label: E
choices.text: They join sister chromatids at the centromere

explanation.summary: The major and minor grooves are protein-binding surfaces used in transcription and replication.
explanation.whyCorrect: The natural twist of the double helix creates a major and a minor groove. These grooves are important because various proteins use them to access the DNA for processes such as transcription and replication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The grooves are structural surface features, not storage sites for spare nucleotides.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The exposed phosphates give DNA a net negative charge; the grooves do not neutralize it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chromatin state changes are driven by methylation and acetylation, not by the helical grooves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sister chromatids are joined at the centromere by cohesins, unrelated to the helical grooves.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: The human genome contains roughly 3 billion base pairs and, when stretched out, is 2 to 3 meters long, yet it must fit within a eukaryotic nucleus about 6 micrometers in diameter. The first step in condensing DNA involves winding it twice around a core of eight histone proteins. What property of histones allows them to bind DNA so tightly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Histones are rich in positively charged amino acids such as lysine and arginine that attract the negatively charged phosphate backbone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Histones are composed mainly of hydrophobic amino acids that bury themselves within the DNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: Histones form covalent bonds with the DNA so it can never be separated

choices.label: D
choices.text: Histones are large aromatic proteins that physically clamp the DNA

choices.label: E
choices.text: Histones carry a strong negative charge that repels the DNA into a coil

explanation.summary: Positively charged histones bind the negatively charged DNA backbone by electrostatic attraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Histones contain an abundance of positively charged amino acids such as lysine and arginine. These bind tightly to the negatively charged phosphate groups of the DNA, allowing DNA to wind around the histone octamer to form a nucleosome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The amino acids that matter are positively charged, not hydrophobic; hydrophobicity does not drive DNA binding here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA-histone interactions are electrostatic (ionic), not covalent; covalent bonds would prevent replication and transcription.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Binding is due to charge attraction, not the bulky aromatic size of the amino acids.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Histones are positively charged; a negative charge would repel rather than bind DNA.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A student is labeling a diagram of DNA packaging. DNA wraps twice around a core of eight histone proteins to form a nucleosome, giving chromatin its 'beads on a string' appearance. A ninth histone protein then secures the DNA onto the core, forming a chromatosome. Which histone protein performs this securing role?

explanation.summary: The linker histone H1 secures DNA onto the histone core to form a chromatosome.
explanation.whyCorrect: The nucleosome core is made of two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4. A ninth histone protein, H1, then secures the DNA around this core, forming the structure called a chromatosome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: H2A is one of the eight core histones of the nucleosome, not the linker that forms the chromatosome.
whyWrongByChoice.B: H2B is a core histone in the octamer, not the securing linker histone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: H3 is a core histone; two copies help form the nucleosome core.
whyWrongByChoice.E: H4 is a core histone; it is part of the octamer, not the linker histone.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A pathologist explains that a region of a patient's genome is transcriptionally silent because it is packaged as highly condensed chromatin that polymerases cannot access. Which term best describes this state of chromatin?

explanation.summary: Heterochromatin is highly condensed and transcriptionally inactive.
explanation.whyCorrect: When DNA is highly condensed it is referred to as heterochromatin. Heterochromatin is inaccessible to polymerases, making it transcriptionally inactive.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Euchromatin is loosely condensed and transcriptionally active, the opposite of what is described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A nucleosome is a single DNA-histone unit, not a description of overall transcriptional accessibility.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A chromatosome is a nucleosome plus H1, a packaging unit, not the condensed silent state itself.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A solenoid fiber is an intermediate coiling structure, not the term for transcriptionally silent condensed chromatin.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A researcher studying epigenetic regulation observes that adding methyl groups to specific DNA sequences recruits repressor proteins and drives chromatin toward a tighter, more condensed configuration. What is the expected effect of this DNA methylation on the affected genes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased gene expression by opening chromatin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased gene expression by promoting condensation into heterochromatin

choices.label: C
choices.text: No effect, because methylation only alters the sugar backbone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conversion of thymine to uracil in the coding strand

explanation.summary: DNA methylation recruits repressors and condenses chromatin, decreasing gene expression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nucleotides can be modified by adding methyl groups to specific DNA sequences. These methyl groups recruit proteins that repress gene expression and facilitate tighter condensation into heterochromatin, thereby decreasing transcription.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acetylation of histone tails opens chromatin and increases expression; DNA methylation does the opposite.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methylation modifies specific base sequences and recruits repressors; it does not merely alter the sugar backbone with no effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Methylation adds methyl groups; it does not convert thymine to uracil.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methylation represses transcription and condenses chromatin rather than triggering strand separation and replication.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: In a study of chromatin dynamics, a compound is applied that adds acetyl groups to the tails of histone subunits. Which outcome is most consistent with histone acetylation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The positive charge of the histones decreases, loosening the coil and opening the chromatin

choices.label: B
choices.text: The DNA forms covalent bonds with the histone tails, permanently silencing the region

choices.label: C
choices.text: The chromatin condenses further into heterochromatin

choices.label: E
choices.text: The centromere dissolves and sister chromatids separate

explanation.summary: Acetylation reduces histone positive charge, decondensing chromatin and opening it for transcription.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acetylation of histone tails decreases the overall positive charge of the histones, weakening their attraction to the negatively charged DNA. This allows the DNA to relax its tight coil, decondensing and opening the chromatin toward the active euchromatin state.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DNA and histones interact through charge attraction, not covalent bonds, and acetylation opens rather than silences the region.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acetylation opens chromatin; it is methylation that promotes condensation into heterochromatin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Base pairing rules (A-T, G-C) are unaffected by histone tail modification.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Centromere function and sister chromatid separation are cell-cycle events unrelated to histone acetylation.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A cytogeneticist prepares a karyotype from a patient's cells and counts the chromosomes. She explains that humans have a diploid genome consisting of pairs of homologous chromosomes. How many chromosomes are present in a typical human somatic cell?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 46 chromosomes: 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes

choices.label: E
choices.text: 48 chromosomes: 23 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes

explanation.summary: Humans have 46 chromosomes: 22 homologous autosome pairs plus one pair of sex chromosomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Humans have diploid genomes with 46 chromosomes: 22 pairs of homologous autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes. Each person inherits a set of 23 chromosomes from the mother and a homologous set of 23 from the father.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 23 is the haploid number contributed by a single gamete, not the total in a somatic cell.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 92 chromatids are present after DNA replication, but the cell still has 46 chromosomes because chromosome number is set by centromere number.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Somatic cells contain sex chromosomes in addition to 44 autosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Humans have 22 autosome pairs plus one sex chromosome pair, totaling 46, not 48.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: During the S phase of the cell cycle, a cell replicates its entire genome. Immediately afterward, each chromosome consists of two identical DNA molecules held together until just before division. What are these two identical DNA molecules called, and what structure connects them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Homologous chromosomes connected at the telomere

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sister chromatids connected at the centromere

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nucleosomes connected by the linker histone

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chromatin fibers connected at the major groove

explanation.summary: Replication yields two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere.
explanation.whyCorrect: After replication, each chromosome consists of two identical DNA molecules called sister chromatids. They are connected at a point called the centromere, which serves as the attachment site for the mitotic spindle that pulls the chromatids apart during division.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Homologous chromosomes are the maternal and paternal copies of a chromosome; they are not the two identical products of replication, and they are not joined at the telomere.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alleles are different versions of a gene, not the replicated DNA molecules, and the nucleolus does not join them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nucleosomes are DNA-histone packaging units, not the replicated chromosome copies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chromatin fibers are a packaging level, and the major groove is a helical surface feature, not a junction between replicated DNA molecules.

questions.topicName: DNA Structure

questions.stem: A researcher grows cells so that their DNA contains a heavy nitrogen isotope, then allows the cells to complete a single round of DNA replication in medium containing only light nitrogen. When the daughter double helices are analyzed, each contains one original heavy strand and one newly synthesized light strand. Which model of DNA replication does this result demonstrate?

explanation.summary: DNA replication follows a semiconservative model in which each daughter helix keeps one parent strand and one new strand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because each strand of the original helix serves as a template for a new complementary strand, every daughter chromosome contains one original parent strand and one newly synthesized daughter strand. A result showing one heavy and one light strand per daughter helix is the hallmark of semiconservative replication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A conservative model would keep the two parent strands together and produce one entirely new daughter helix, not a hybrid of one old and one new strand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A dispersive model would scatter old and new segments along both strands, so no strand would be entirely heavy or entirely light.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bidirectional refers to fork movement from the origin, not to how parent and daughter strands are partitioned; the partitioning shown here is still semiconservative.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no random-strand model; strand inheritance in replication is defined and predictable, not random.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: During initiation of DNA replication, helicase and its helper proteins preferentially begin separating the parent strands at regions of the helix that are rich in adenine-thymine base pairs rather than guanine-cytosine base pairs. Which of the following best explains why A-T rich regions are easier to separate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A-T pairs are held together by two hydrogen bonds, whereas G-C pairs have three

choices.label: B
choices.text: A-T pairs are joined by covalent phosphodiester bonds that helicase cleaves quickly

choices.label: C
choices.text: A-T pairs lack hydrogen bonds entirely and therefore fall apart on their own

choices.label: D
choices.text: G-C pairs are stabilized by single-stranded binding proteins that block helicase

choices.label: E
choices.text: A-T pairs are located only at the origin of replication, so helicase cannot reach G-C pairs

explanation.summary: A-T base pairs are held by two hydrogen bonds while G-C base pairs are held by three, making A-T rich DNA easier to unwind.
explanation.whyCorrect: Helicase severs the hydrogen bonds between paired bases to separate the strands. Because A-T pairs have only two hydrogen bonds compared with three for G-C pairs, less energy is needed to open A-T rich regions, so helicase and its helper proteins favor them.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hydrogen bonds, not phosphodiester bonds, hold complementary strands together; phosphodiester bonds link nucleotides within a single strand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A-T pairs are held by hydrogen bonds; they simply have fewer of them, but they do not lack them altogether.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Single-stranded binding proteins bind exposed single strands after separation to prevent reannealing; they are not responsible for stabilizing intact G-C pairs against helicase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A-T pairs are distributed throughout the genome, not confined to the origin; the ease of separation reflects hydrogen bond number, not location.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: In an in vitro system, purified DNA polymerase is added to a single-stranded DNA template with abundant free nucleotides, yet no new DNA is synthesized. Synthesis begins only after a second enzyme is added that generates a short segment with a free 3'-hydroxyl group. Which enzyme was added, and what does it produce?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ligase, which produces a phosphodiester bond

choices.label: B
choices.text: Topoisomerase, which produces a single-stranded nick

choices.label: C
choices.text: Primase, which produces a short RNA primer

choices.label: D
choices.text: Helicase, which produces a replication bubble

choices.label: E
choices.text: Flap endonuclease, which produces a trimmed 3' flap

explanation.summary: DNA polymerase cannot start on a bare template and requires a primer with a free 3'-OH, which primase supplies as a short RNA segment.
explanation.whyCorrect: DNA polymerases can only add nucleotides to an existing polynucleotide that has a 3'-OH; they cannot initiate synthesis on a naked template. Primase lays down a short RNA primer that provides this 3'-OH, allowing DNA polymerase to bind and begin elongating the new strand.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ligase seals gaps between existing fragments by forming phosphodiester bonds; it does not create the initial primer needed to start synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Topoisomerase nicks and reseals DNA to relieve supercoiling; the transient nick does not serve as a starting primer for polymerase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Helicase separates the strands to form the replication bubble but does not provide the 3'-OH primer that polymerase requires.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flap endonuclease trims a displaced flap during Okazaki fragment processing; it does not generate a starting primer.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: As helicase unwinds the double helix at the replication fork, the two exposed parent strands are complementary and tend to snap back together into a duplex, which would block the replicative enzymes. Which of the following proteins directly prevents this reannealing?

explanation.summary: Single-stranded binding proteins coat the exposed nucleotides of separated parent strands to keep them from reannealing.
explanation.whyCorrect: Once helicase separates the strands, single-stranded binding proteins bind the exposed nitrogenous bases, providing a physical barrier that prevents the complementary strands from reassociating and stops them from interacting with free nucleotides. They are readily displaced by DNA polymerase and primase so synthesis is not blocked.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Topoisomerase relieves supercoiling tension downstream of the fork; it does not coat single strands to prevent reannealing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA ligase seals gaps between Okazaki fragments and has no role in keeping separated parent strands apart.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primase synthesizes RNA primers; it does not prevent the parent strands from reannealing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The PCNA sliding clamp keeps polymerase anchored to the template to boost processivity, not to prevent strand reannealing.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: As helicase unwinds DNA, excessive supercoiling builds up ahead of the replication fork. An ATP-dependent enzyme relieves this tension by grabbing two portions of the same supercoiled helix, introducing a double-stranded break at one location, and passing the other segment through the break before resealing it. Which enzyme is described?

explanation.summary: Topoisomerase II is ATP-dependent and relieves supercoiling by creating a double-stranded break and passing another segment of helix through it.
explanation.whyCorrect: Type II topoisomerases exist as homodimeric or heterotetrameric ATP-dependent enzymes. They grab two portions of the same supercoiled helix, introduce a double-stranded break at one location, and pass the other portion through that break before resealing, which relaxes the supercoil.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Topoisomerase I is a monomeric, ATP-independent enzyme that makes only a transient single-stranded break, allowing the helix to rotate around the intact strand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Helicase separates strands by breaking hydrogen bonds and is the source of the supercoiling tension, not the enzyme that relieves it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primase synthesizes RNA primers and has no role in relaxing supercoils.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flap endonuclease trims displaced flaps during Okazaki fragment processing and does not act on supercoiling.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: A student is tracing how DNA polymerase moves during synthesis of a new daughter strand. Because DNA strands are complementary and antiparallel, in order to build the new strand in the 5' to 3' direction the polymerase must travel along the template strand in which direction?

choices.label: D
choices.text: In whichever direction the RNA primer points, independent of polarity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Always toward the nearest origin of replication regardless of strand polarity

explanation.summary: Because strands are antiparallel, synthesizing the new strand 5' to 3' requires the polymerase to read the template 3' to 5'.
explanation.whyCorrect: DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides to the 3' end, so new strands are always synthesized in the 5' to 3' direction. Since the two strands run antiparallel, the polymerase must move along the template strand in the 3' to 5' direction to accomplish this.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Moving 5' to 3' along the template would require building the new strand 3' to 5', which DNA polymerase cannot do.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A single polymerase reads the template in one fixed direction; it cannot move both ways at once.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Directionality is dictated by the antiparallel, 5'-to-3' chemistry of synthesis, not by the orientation of the primer alone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Polymerase direction is set by strand polarity and the 5'-to-3' rule, not by proximity to an origin.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: At each replication fork, one new strand is made continuously while the other is assembled from many short segments called Okazaki fragments, each requiring its own RNA primer. Which fundamental property of DNA is the underlying reason the lagging strand must be synthesized in this semi-discontinuous manner?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The 3' to 5' polymerization style of DNA Pol epsilon

choices.label: B
choices.text: The RNA primase activity of a subunit of DNA Pol alpha

choices.label: C
choices.text: The poor processivity and rapid dissociation of DNA Pol delta from templates

choices.label: D
choices.text: The complementary, antiparallel nature of the DNA molecule

choices.label: E
choices.text: The presence of multiple origins of replication on the chromosome

explanation.summary: The antiparallel nature of DNA means only one strand can be copied continuously, forcing the other to be made discontinuously as Okazaki fragments.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because DNA polymerases synthesize only in the 5' to 3' direction and the two strands are antiparallel, at any given fork only one strand (the leading strand) can be made continuously. The other template is exposed in the wrong orientation, so its new strand must be built in short Okazaki fragments as more template becomes available.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DNA polymerases synthesize in the 5' to 3' direction, not 3' to 5'; this option misstates polymerase chemistry.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The RNA primase subunit permits semi-discontinuous synthesis by laying primers, but it is not the underlying cause of the discontinuity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA Pol delta actually has high processivity because it associates with a sliding clamp, so poor processivity is not the reason.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Multiple origins speed up replication of long chromosomes but do not explain why one strand is made discontinuously.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: DNA Pol delta and Pol epsilon are the primary replicative polymerases in eukaryotes and remain bound to the template long enough to copy long stretches of DNA accurately. This high processivity is most directly explained by their association with which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The PCNA sliding clamp, which forms a ring around the DNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: The RNA primase subunit of DNA Pol alpha

choices.label: C
choices.text: Single-stranded binding proteins coating the template

choices.label: D
choices.text: Topoisomerase acting as a swivel ahead of the fork

explanation.summary: The proliferating cell nuclear antigen (PCNA) sliding clamp encircles the DNA and keeps Pol delta and Pol epsilon attached, giving them high processivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Processivity describes how long a polymerase stays bound to the template. Pol delta and Pol epsilon achieve high processivity by associating with the PCNA sliding clamp, which forms a ring around the DNA and literally keeps the polymerase attached during replication.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The primase subunit of Pol alpha initiates synthesis with RNA primers but does not anchor Pol delta or Pol epsilon to the template.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Single-stranded binding proteins prevent reannealing of separated strands and are displaced by the polymerase; they do not confer processivity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Topoisomerase relieves supercoiling ahead of the fork and does not tether the polymerase to DNA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flap endonuclease trims displaced flaps during fragment processing and has no role in polymerase processivity.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: Eukaryotic DNA replication begins with a unique multi-subunit polymerase. One of its subunits has RNA primase activity and lays the primer needed to start synthesis on the leading strand and for each Okazaki fragment, but the enzyme detaches after adding only about 20 nucleotides. Which polymerase is described?

explanation.summary: DNA Pol alpha is the multi-subunit initiating polymerase; its primase subunit lays the primer, but it has low processivity and stays bound for only about 20 nucleotides.
explanation.whyCorrect: Replication starts with DNA Pol alpha, a unique multi-subunit enzyme. One subunit has RNA primase activity and generates the primer that allows initiation on the leading strand and for each Okazaki fragment. Pol alpha has low processivity and stays attached to the template for only about 20 nucleotides before Pol delta and Pol epsilon take over.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DNA Pol delta is recruited after initiation to complete the Okazaki fragments and has high processivity, not primase activity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA Pol epsilon completes the leading strand with high processivity and does not carry the primase subunit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Topoisomerase I relaxes supercoils and is not a polymerase and does not lay primers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DNA ligase seals gaps between fragments and does not initiate synthesis or synthesize primers.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: In eukaryotes, as DNA Pol delta synthesizes an Okazaki fragment it does not stop when it reaches the previous RNA primer; instead it displaces the primer and a short 5' segment of the previous fragment, creating a single-stranded flap. Which enzyme removes this flap so the fragments can be joined?

explanation.summary: Strand-displacement synthesis by Pol delta creates a flap that a special flap endonuclease trims before ligase seals the gap.
explanation.whyCorrect: When Pol delta displaces the RNA primer and a short 5' segment of the previous Okazaki fragment, it produces a flap that requires resection. A special flap endonuclease trims this flap, which then allows ligase to seal the gap between the fragments.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DNA ligase seals the gap only after the flap has been trimmed; it does not cut the flap itself.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Helicase unwinds the parent duplex at the fork and does not act on displaced flaps during fragment processing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Topoisomerase II relieves supercoiling by cutting and passing helix segments and does not trim lagging strand flaps.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Single-stranded binding proteins prevent reannealing of parent strands and do not remove flaps.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: After the RNA primers on the lagging strand are removed and the flaps are trimmed, adjacent Okazaki fragments remain as separate segments with nicks in the sugar-phosphate backbone between them. Which enzyme joins these fragments into a continuous strand, and by forming what type of bond?

choices.label: A
choices.text: DNA ligase, by forming phosphodiester bonds

choices.label: B
choices.text: DNA polymerase, by forming hydrogen bonds

explanation.summary: DNA ligase seals the gaps between Okazaki fragments by forming phosphodiester bonds in the backbone.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ligase creates phosphodiester bonds between adjacent Okazaki fragments to seal the remaining gaps, converting the discontinuous lagging strand segments into one continuous daughter strand. This is the final joining step of lagging strand synthesis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DNA polymerase adds nucleotides to build strands; it does not seal the nick between fragments, and hydrogen bonds link complementary bases rather than joining backbone segments.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Primase makes RNA primers and does not seal gaps between DNA fragments.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Topoisomerase relieves supercoiling by nicking and resealing; it does not ligate separate Okazaki fragments, and base-pair hydrogen bonds are not what join the backbone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Helicase separates strands and plays no role in joining fragments.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: During synthesis, DNA Pol epsilon occasionally inserts a nucleotide that does not correctly pair with the template. Before continuing polymerization, the enzyme detects the mismatch, removes the incorrectly paired nucleotide, and then inserts the correct one. Which intrinsic polymerase activity is responsible for this correction?

explanation.summary: The 3' to 5' exonuclease proofreading activity of Pol delta and Pol epsilon removes a mismatched nucleotide before synthesis continues.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both primary replicative polymerases have proofreading capability provided by 3' to 5' exonuclease activity. When a mispairing is identified, this activity removes the incorrectly paired nucleotide from the 3' end so the correct nucleotide can be inserted, giving these polymerases very high fidelity and an estimated mutation rate below one in one hundred million base pairs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The 5' to 3' polymerase activity adds nucleotides to build the strand; it is the activity that can introduce the error, not correct it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: RNA primase activity generates primers and does not remove mismatched nucleotides.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Helicase activity unwinds the duplex and is not part of proofreading.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The sliding clamp increases processivity by anchoring the polymerase but does not itself excise mismatched nucleotides.

questions.topicName: DNA Replication

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man is prescribed an antibiotic that treats his bacterial infection by disrupting the translation of mRNA into protein. After reading the drug information sheet, he worries the drug will also shut down protein synthesis in his own cells. Which of the following best explains why the antibiotic will not interfere with translation in humans?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Human cells synthesize proteins in the nucleus, where the antibiotic cannot penetrate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bacterial ribosomes (30S and 50S subunits forming a 70S ribosome) differ from human ribosomes (40S and 60S forming an 80S ribosome), and the drug binds only the bacterial subunits

choices.label: C
choices.text: Human cells lack ribosomes and instead translate mRNA using the spliceosome

choices.label: D
choices.text: The 5' cap on human mRNA prevents any antibiotic from binding the transcript

choices.label: E
choices.text: Human mRNA is degraded by the antibiotic before it can be translated

explanation.summary: Prokaryotic and eukaryotic ribosomes are built from different subunits, so ribosome-targeting antibiotics are selective for bacteria.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prokaryotic ribosomes are made of 30S and 50S subunits that combine into a 70S ribosome, whereas human ribosomes use 40S and 60S subunits that combine into an 80S ribosome. Because the antibiotic binds only the subunits found in bacteria, it cannot affect human ribosomes or human protein synthesis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Eukaryotic translation occurs in the cytoplasm on free or RER-bound ribosomes, not in the nucleus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: All human cells contain ribosomes; the spliceosome removes introns from pre-mRNA and does not translate mRNA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The 5' cap aids export, protects against exonucleases, and initiates translation, but it does not block antibiotics; selectivity comes from ribosome differences.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The antibiotic targets bacterial ribosomes and does not degrade human mRNA.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: A researcher studying the initiation of transcription in a human cell line wants to identify a proximal regulatory sequence that recruits transcription factors and RNA polymerase to begin transcription. Which of the following is such a proximal regulatory element?

explanation.summary: The CAAT and TATA boxes are proximal promoter elements that initiate eukaryotic transcription.
explanation.whyCorrect: CAAT and TATA boxes are proximal regulatory sequences located close to the gene that recruit transcription factors and RNA polymerase II to initiate transcription. The CAAT box lies roughly 70-80 nucleotides upstream of the start site.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AUG is the start codon for translation, not a transcription regulatory sequence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An enhancer is a distal regulatory sequence that can act thousands of base pairs away, not a proximal one.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The Pribnow box is a prokaryotic promoter element, not a eukaryotic proximal sequence.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The 5' UTR is a segment of mRNA upstream of the start codon and does not initiate transcription.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: While mapping the regulatory landscape of a gene, an investigator identifies a DNA sequence located roughly 12,000 base pairs downstream of the gene that binds activating transcription factors and markedly increases the amount of the corresponding protein produced. Which of the following best describes this sequence?

explanation.summary: Enhancers are distal regulatory sequences that recruit activating transcription factors to upregulate expression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Enhancers are distal regulatory sequences that can lie thousands of base pairs upstream or downstream of a gene. They recruit activating transcription factors that promote recruitment of RNA polymerase II, upregulating expression of the target gene and its protein.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A silencer is also distal, but it recruits repressor factors and downregulates expression rather than increasing it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The TATA box is a proximal promoter element about 25-30 nucleotides upstream, not a distal element thousands of base pairs away.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The polyadenylation signal (AAUAAA) marks the site for poly(A) tail addition on pre-mRNA and does not increase transcription.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The CAAT box is a proximal promoter element roughly 70-80 nucleotides upstream, not a distal enhancer.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: A student observes that the mRNA sequence produced from a gene exactly matches the coding (sense) strand of the DNA, aside from one type of base. Which of the following statements about transcription is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: RNA polymerase II uses the coding strand as its template

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mRNA is identical to the coding strand except that uracil replaces thymine

choices.label: C
choices.text: RNA is synthesized in the 3' to 5' direction

choices.label: D
choices.text: The template strand is also called the sense strand

choices.label: E
choices.text: RNA polymerase II travels 5' to 3' along the template strand

explanation.summary: The RNA transcript matches the coding (sense) strand with uracil in place of thymine, while the template (antisense) strand is read.
explanation.whyCorrect: RNA polymerase II reads the template (antisense) strand to produce a transcript that corresponds to the other strand of the helix, the coding (sense) strand, except that uracil is paired with adenine instead of thymidine. Thus the mRNA has the same sequence as the coding strand with U substituted for T.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The polymerase uses the template (antisense) strand as its template, not the coding strand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The RNA transcript is built 5' to 3' by adding nucleotides to its 3' end.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The template strand is the antisense strand; the coding strand is the sense strand.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RNA polymerase II moves 3' to 5' along the template strand while the RNA is elongated 5' to 3'.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: A cell line carries a mutation in the enzyme that adds the 7-methylguanosine cap to the 5' end of pre-mRNA, so newly made transcripts remain uncapped. Which of the following is the most likely direct consequence for these transcripts once they reach the cytoplasm?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure to remove introns from the transcript

choices.label: D
choices.text: Introduction of a premature stop codon

choices.label: E
choices.text: Conversion of the mRNA back into DNA

explanation.summary: The 5' cap protects mRNA from 5' exonucleases; without it, the transcript is rapidly degraded.
explanation.whyCorrect: One key function of the 5' cap is to protect mRNA from degradation by 5' exonucleases in the cytoplasm that remove nucleotides from the ends of polynucleotides. An uncapped transcript loses this protection and is degraded more readily; the cap also normally aids nuclear export and translation initiation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Intron removal is carried out by the spliceosome and is independent of the 5' cap.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The poly(A) tail is added at the 3' end by poly(A) polymerase and does not depend on 5' capping.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Capping does not alter the coding sequence, so it cannot introduce a premature stop codon.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reverse transcription of mRNA to DNA requires reverse transcriptase and is unrelated to loss of the cap.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: A patient's cells harbor a mutation that changes the pre-mRNA sequence 5'-AAUAAA-3' near the 3' end of a transcript. Which of the following steps of mRNA maturation is most directly impaired?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cleavage of the pre-mRNA and addition of the poly(A) tail

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognition of the start codon by the 40S subunit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removal of introns by the spliceosome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Charging of tRNA by aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase

explanation.summary: The 5'-AAUAAA-3' polyadenylation signal directs cleavage and poly(A) tail addition at the 3' end.
explanation.whyCorrect: The polyadenylation signal (5'-AAUAAA-3') near the 3' end of the pre-mRNA signals a nearby downstream cleavage site; after cleavage, poly(A) polymerase uses ATP to add 40-250 adenosine nucleotides. A mutation in this signal impairs cleavage and poly(A) tail addition, destabilizing the mRNA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The 5' cap is added at the opposite end of the transcript and is independent of the AAUAAA signal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Start codon recognition occurs during translation initiation and does not depend on the polyadenylation signal.
