whyWrongByChoice.D: Splicing is directed by intron donor/acceptor sites and the branch point, not by the AAUAAA sequence.
whyWrongByChoice.E: tRNA charging occurs in the cytoplasm and is unrelated to processing of the 3' end of pre-mRNA.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: A point mutation destroys the conserved GU dinucleotide at the 5' end of an intron in a pre-mRNA transcript. Which of the following is the most likely consequence for the mature transcript?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The intron is retained because the spliceosome cannot recognize the 5' donor site

choices.label: B
choices.text: The 5' cap fails to be added to the transcript

choices.label: C
choices.text: The poly(A) tail is not added to the 3' end

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transcription terminates prematurely at the mutation

choices.label: E
choices.text: The transcript is reverse transcribed into DNA

explanation.summary: Each intron has a 5' donor site (5'-GU) recognized by the spliceosome; disrupting it prevents proper splicing and causes intron retention.
explanation.whyCorrect: Each intron has a 5' donor site containing 5'-GU and a 3' acceptor site containing AG-3'. The spliceosome first cleaves at the 5' splice site to form the lariat; if the GU donor site is mutated, the spliceosome cannot recognize and cut the site, so the intron is not removed and is retained in the transcript.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Capping occurs at the 5' end of the whole transcript and is independent of individual intron splice sites.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The poly(A) tail depends on the AAUAAA polyadenylation signal, not on an intron donor site.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The donor-site mutation affects splicing of the finished transcript, not termination of transcription.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reverse transcription requires reverse transcriptase and does not result from a splice-site mutation.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: A single B-lymphocyte gene gives rise to two structurally related immunoglobulin proteins: a membrane-bound form and a secreted form. Both are encoded by the same gene, yet they differ in their final domains. Which of the following processes best explains how one gene produces these different but related proteins?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A silent mutation in the coding exon

choices.label: D
choices.text: Addition of a longer poly(A) tail

explanation.summary: Alternative splicing lets a single gene generate a range of unique but structurally related proteins.
explanation.whyCorrect: Organisms use alternative splicing to produce a range of unique but structurally related proteins, such as a transmembrane versus a secreted immunoglobulin. The same gene can be alternatively spliced into different mRNA molecules that yield the different protein isoforms.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Reverse transcription copies RNA into DNA and does not generate protein isoforms from one gene.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A silent mutation does not change the amino acid sequence and cannot create two distinct protein forms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Poly(A) tail length affects mRNA stability, not which protein domains are produced.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Human cells use 80S ribosomes; ribosome type does not create alternative isoforms from a gene.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: In a cell-free translation system, an aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase mistakenly attaches arginine to a tRNA whose anticodon is specific for valine. The mischarged tRNA is then used in an otherwise normal ribosome. Which of the following is most likely to occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The arginine will be incorporated at positions where valine is called for

choices.label: B
choices.text: The arginine will be incorporated at random positions along the peptide

choices.label: C
choices.text: The arginine will be chemically converted into valine before incorporation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The ribosome will detect the mismatched arginine and prevent its incorporation

choices.label: E
choices.text: The tRNA will spontaneously dissociate from the arginine before reaching the ribosome

explanation.summary: Ribosomes read the tRNA anticodon, not the attached amino acid, so a mischarged tRNA inserts the wrong amino acid at the codon it recognizes.
explanation.whyCorrect: If a tRNA is charged with an incorrect amino acid, it still enters the ribosome and incorporates that amino acid according to its anticodon. Because the tRNA's anticodon is specific to the valine codon, the arginine will be incorporated wherever valine is called for.

whyWrongByChoice.B: tRNAs are highly specific to the codon code via their anticodon, so incorporation is not random.
whyWrongByChoice.C: No cellular process spontaneously converts one amino acid into another after charging.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ribosomes lack proofreading of amino acid identity; they rely on the accuracy of tRNA charging.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A charged tRNA is unlikely to spontaneously dissociate from its attached amino acid.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: During initiation of eukaryotic translation, the small ribosomal subunit and a charged tRNA scan the mRNA to locate the start codon before the large subunit joins. Which of the following statements about this initiation step is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The first amino acid is always methionine, and its charged tRNA occupies the P site

choices.label: B
choices.text: The first amino acid is always methionine, and its charged tRNA enters at the A site

choices.label: C
choices.text: Translation begins at the first UAA codon encountered from the 5' end

choices.label: D
choices.text: The 60S subunit binds the mRNA first and scans for the start codon

choices.label: E
choices.text: The initiator tRNA is charged with leucine encoded by the AUG codon

explanation.summary: Translation starts at AUG with methionine, and the initiator Met-tRNA uniquely enters at the P site.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because there is only one start codon (AUG) in the genetic code, the first amino acid incorporated is always methionine, so met-tRNA is always used to initiate translation. Uniquely, this initiator tRNA enters the ribosome at the P site rather than the A site.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The initiator met-tRNA is the exception that enters at the P site, not the A site.
whyWrongByChoice.C: UAA is a stop codon; translation begins at the AUG start codon, not a stop codon.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The small 40S subunit recognizes the 5' cap and scans for the start codon; the 60S subunit joins afterward.
whyWrongByChoice.E: AUG codes for methionine, not leucine.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: During the elongation phase of translation, a charged tRNA carrying the next amino acid must enter the ribosome so that a peptide bond can form. At which ribosomal site does an incoming charged tRNA normally enter, and what happens there?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The A site, which accepts the incoming charged tRNA matching the next codon

choices.label: B
choices.text: The P site, which ejects uncharged tRNAs from the ribosome

choices.label: C
choices.text: The E site, which holds the tRNA carrying the growing peptide chain

choices.label: D
choices.text: The A site, where uncharged tRNAs exit after donating their amino acid

choices.label: E
choices.text: The E site, which is the entry point for all incoming charged tRNAs

explanation.summary: The A (aminoacyl) site is where charged tRNAs enter; the P site holds the growing peptide, and the E site ejects uncharged tRNAs.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ribosome has three sites. The A site is where charged tRNAs enter the ribosome, matching the next codon. The P site holds the tRNA with the growing peptide chain, and the E site is where uncharged tRNAs are ejected after their amino acid has been incorporated.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The P site holds the tRNA bearing the growing peptide chain; ejection of uncharged tRNA occurs at the E site.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The E site holds uncharged tRNAs just before ejection, not the tRNA carrying the growing peptide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Uncharged tRNAs exit from the E site, not the A site; the A site accepts incoming charged tRNAs.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The E site is the exit site; incoming charged tRNAs enter at the A site.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: SARS-CoV-2, a positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus, replicates its genome inside a human cell without ever converting its RNA into DNA. Which of the following enzymes, representing an exception to the central dogma, does this virus use to copy its genome?

explanation.summary: Positive-sense ssRNA viruses like SARS-CoV-2 use an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase to make RNA from an RNA template.
explanation.whyCorrect: Some viruses, but not eukaryotes, have an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase that uses RNA as a template to make more RNA. SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19, uses this enzyme to replicate, as do all positive-sense single-stranded RNA viruses. This is one of the two exceptions to the unidirectional central dogma.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Reverse transcriptase makes DNA from an RNA template and is used by retroviruses like HIV, not by SARS-CoV-2 for genome replication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA polymerase synthesizes DNA and is used in DNA replication, not RNA-to-RNA copying.
whyWrongByChoice.D: RNA polymerase II transcribes DNA into pre-mRNA and does not copy RNA into RNA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase charges tRNAs during translation and does not replicate viral genomes.

questions.topicName: The Central Dogma: DNA to RNA to Proteins

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl is enrolled in a research study. Sequencing of a gene reveals a single-nucleotide change in the third position of one codon, yet the amino acid sequence of the encoded protein is completely unchanged and her phenotype is normal. Which of the following best explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mutated codon still specifies the same amino acid because the genetic code is redundant

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mutation introduced a premature stop codon that was later repaired

choices.label: C
choices.text: The mutation shifted the reading frame back into the correct position

choices.label: D
choices.text: The change substituted an amino acid with very different chemical properties

choices.label: E
choices.text: The mutation created a new splice acceptor site within the exon

explanation.summary: A silent (synonymous) mutation changes a nucleotide but not the amino acid because of codon redundancy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The genetic code has 64 codons but only 20 amino acids, so multiple codons can specify the same amino acid. When a point mutation produces a codon that still codes for the original amino acid, it is a silent or synonymous mutation with no effect on protein structure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A premature stop codon is a nonsense mutation and would truncate the protein, changing the product rather than leaving it identical.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A single point mutation does not delete or insert nucleotides, so the reading frame is not shifted.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Substituting an amino acid with different properties describes a non-conservative missense mutation, which would change the sequence.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Creating a splice site would alter mRNA processing and the final protein, contrary to the unchanged sequence described.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: DZ, a 10-year-old boy, is evaluated in the cystic fibrosis clinic. His attending explains that his disease is due to a point mutation in the CFTR gene that produces a premature termination codon (UAG) within the coding sequence. Which of the following is the most likely direct consequence of this mutation on the CFTR protein?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Production of a full-length protein with a single altered amino acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Premature termination of translation yielding a truncated, non-functional protein

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insertion of an extra amino acid without a change in reading frame

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retention of an intron in the mature mRNA

choices.label: E
choices.text: Overexpression of a normal protein at abnormally high levels

explanation.summary: A nonsense mutation creates a premature stop codon that terminates translation early, producing a truncated protein.
explanation.whyCorrect: A premature termination codon (UAA, UGA, or UAG) causes the ribosome to stop translation before the full protein is made. Most truncated proteins lack the active portion and are non-functional; in cystic fibrosis this yields a dysfunctional CFTR protein and thick, sticky mucus.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A single altered amino acid in a full-length protein describes a missense mutation, not a nonsense mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inserting an amino acid without a frameshift describes an in-frame insertion, not a premature stop codon.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intron retention results from a splice donor site mutation, not from a nonsense point mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Overexpression of a normal product describes a gain-of-function mutation, the opposite of premature termination.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A newborn with sickle cell disease has a point mutation in the beta-globin gene in which a codon for glutamate (an acidic, negatively charged amino acid) is changed to a codon for valine (a nonpolar amino acid). Which of the following best classifies this mutation?

explanation.summary: A missense change to an amino acid with very different chemical properties is a non-conservative missense mutation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The mutated codon encodes a different amino acid, making it a missense (nonsynonymous) mutation. Because valine (nonpolar) has very different properties than the original glutamate (acidic, charged), it is classified as non-conservative and often has a greater effect on protein function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A silent mutation leaves the amino acid unchanged; here the amino acid is replaced.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A conservative missense mutation substitutes an amino acid with similar properties (same charge or acidity), not a charged residue for a nonpolar one.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A nonsense mutation introduces a stop codon; here a functional amino acid is still incorporated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A frameshift requires insertion or deletion of nucleotides, not a single base substitution.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A researcher inserts a single extra nucleotide into the coding region of a gene, downstream of the start codon. When the mutant gene is translated, the amino acid sequence beyond the insertion is entirely different from the normal protein. Which of the following best describes this type of mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A silent mutation with no downstream effect

choices.label: B
choices.text: A conservative missense mutation affecting one codon

choices.label: C
choices.text: A frameshift mutation that alters every downstream codon

choices.label: D
choices.text: An in-frame insertion that adds a single amino acid

choices.label: E
choices.text: A splice acceptor mutation causing exon skipping

explanation.summary: Inserting a non-triplet number of nucleotides shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream codons.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a single nucleotide (a non-triplet segment) is inserted into the coding region, a frameshift mutation occurs. Every downstream codon is read in a new frame, so the entire sequence past the insertion is altered and a premature stop codon may appear.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A silent mutation changes a base but not the amino acid, and it does not affect downstream codons.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A missense mutation changes only a single codon, not the entire downstream sequence.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adding a single amino acid in-frame requires an insertion of three nucleotides, which would not shift the frame.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Exon skipping results from a splice acceptor site mutation, not from inserting a nucleotide in the coding region.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A gene is found to have a deletion of exactly three consecutive nucleotides within its coding region. Compared with the normal protein, the encoded protein is missing one amino acid but the rest of its downstream sequence is unchanged. Which of the following best explains why a frameshift did not occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The deletion was located in a noncoding region

choices.label: B
choices.text: The deletion involved a multiple of three nucleotides, keeping the reading frame intact

choices.label: C
choices.text: The deletion created a new stop codon that halted translation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The deletion converted the codon to one for the same amino acid

choices.label: E
choices.text: The deletion occurred in the splice donor site

explanation.summary: Deleting a multiple of three nucleotides removes whole codons and preserves the reading frame, avoiding a frameshift.
explanation.whyCorrect: Any deletion or insertion that is a multiple of three keeps the downstream sequence in the same reading frame, so only a few amino acids are removed or added while the ribosome stays in frame. This is why a three-nucleotide deletion (as in CFTR delta-F508) removes a single amino acid without frameshifting.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The stem states the deletion is within the coding region, and a noncoding location would not remove an amino acid from the protein.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A new stop codon would truncate the protein, not simply remove one amino acid with an otherwise normal downstream sequence.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Converting a codon to one for the same amino acid describes a silent point mutation, not a three-nucleotide deletion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A splice donor site deletion would disrupt intron removal, not cleanly delete one amino acid in frame.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A patient has a mutation that alters the 5'-GU start sequence at the boundary of an intron in a gene. Analysis of the mature mRNA shows that the intron adjacent to the mutation is incorrectly included in the final transcript. This start sequence corresponds to which splicing element, and what is the expected result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Splice acceptor site; the adjacent exon is skipped

choices.label: B
choices.text: Splice donor site; the adjacent intron is retained

choices.label: C
choices.text: Splice acceptor site; a cryptic exon is inserted

choices.label: D
choices.text: Splice donor site; the reading frame is unaffected and no change occurs

choices.label: E
choices.text: Branch point; two adjacent exons are fused

explanation.summary: The 5'-GU intron start is the splice donor site; disrupting it leads to intron retention.
explanation.whyCorrect: Introns are typically flanked by 5'-GU (splice donor) and AG-3' (splice acceptor) sequences. If the donor (start) sequence is altered, the spliceosome fails to remove that intron correctly and the entire intron is incorrectly included in the mature mRNA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Exon skipping results from disruption of the AG-3' acceptor site, not the 5'-GU donor site.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cryptic exon insertion requires creation of new splice sequences within an intron, not simple loss of a donor site.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Retaining an intron changes the mRNA sequence, so translation and the protein are affected, not unchanged.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The 5'-GU sequence is the donor site, not the branch point, and the described outcome is intron retention.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: In a different patient, a mutation destroys the AG-3' end sequence of an intron. The spliceosome scans past the destroyed site to the next available end sequence. Which of the following outcomes is most likely?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An entire exon is removed from the final mRNA (exon skipping)

choices.label: B
choices.text: An intron is retained in the final mRNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: A silent mutation with no effect on the mRNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: A single amino acid substitution in the protein

choices.label: E
choices.text: Overexpression of the normal gene product

explanation.summary: Loss of the AG-3' splice acceptor site causes the spliceosome to skip to the next end sequence, removing an exon.
explanation.whyCorrect: The AG-3' end sequence is the splice acceptor site. If it is altered, the spliceosome scans for the next end sequence in the pre-mRNA, which results in an entire exon being removed from the final mRNA.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Intron retention follows loss of the 5'-GU donor (start) site, not the AG-3' acceptor site.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A splice site mutation changes mRNA processing, so it is not silent with no effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A single amino acid substitution is a missense point mutation, unrelated to loss of a splice acceptor site.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Overexpression describes a gain-of-function regulatory change, not a splicing defect.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with cafe-au-lait spots, a large head, and several bones affected by fibrous dysplasia and skin patches following developmental lines; her parents and siblings are unaffected. Genetic testing shows the causative mutation is present in affected tissues but absent from her blood leukocytes and germ cells. Which of the following best describes this type of mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A germline mutation present in every cell of the body

choices.label: B
choices.text: A somatic mutation limited to a subset of tissues and not inherited

choices.label: C
choices.text: A frameshift mutation inherited from an affected parent

choices.label: D
choices.text: A silent mutation with no clinical consequence

choices.label: E
choices.text: A splice site mutation affecting all cells equally

explanation.summary: A mutation present only in some tissues and not in germ cells is a somatic mutation, exemplified by McCune-Albright syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Somatic mutations occur in non-reproductive tissue and are not passed to offspring. When they occur early in development they can cause a widespread but patchy phenotype, as in McCune-Albright syndrome affecting bone, skin, and endocrine tissues. The mutation is seen only in the individual and not inherited.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A germline mutation would be present in every cell, including blood and germ cells, which contradicts the testing results.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The family history is negative and the mutation is not in germ cells, so it was not inherited from a parent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A silent mutation has no clinical consequence, but this patient has a defined disease phenotype.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mutation is described as present in only a subset of tissues, not affecting all cells equally.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy has multiple cafe-au-lait spots and benign tumors growing along nerves in the skin and brain due to a mutation in the NF-1 gene. His father is similarly affected. The mutation is detectable in every cell type tested, including his sperm. Which of the following best characterizes this mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A somatic mutation confined to neural tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: A germline mutation present in all cells and heritable

choices.label: C
choices.text: A silent mutation that does not affect the phenotype

choices.label: D
choices.text: A mutation that arose after birth and is not passed to offspring

choices.label: E
choices.text: A mutation present only in tumor cells

explanation.summary: A mutation present in every cell, including germ cells, and passed through a family is a germline mutation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Germline mutations occur in the tissue that produces sex cells (ova or sperm), so they are passed to offspring and are present in every cell of the affected individual. Neurofibromatosis type 1, caused by an NF-1 gene mutation, is a classic inherited (germline) disorder with cafe-au-lait spots and nerve-associated tumors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A somatic mutation would be confined to certain tissues and not found in germ cells, but this mutation is in every cell.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A silent mutation does not change the phenotype, yet this patient clearly has disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The mutation is inherited from his father and present in sperm, so it is heritable rather than acquired after birth.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mutation is present in every cell type tested, not only in tumor cells.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A child with a neurodevelopmental disorder is found to have a deletion of one copy of a set of genes at 7q11.23, leaving one normal functional allele of each gene. The remaining single allele produces only about 50% of the normal amount of gene product, which is insufficient for a normal phenotype. Which of the following best describes this mechanism of disease?

explanation.summary: When 50% of gene product from a single working allele is not enough for a normal phenotype, the mechanism is haploinsufficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Haploinsufficiency occurs when one functional copy of a gene, producing about 50% of the normal product, cannot generate a normal phenotype. This underlies Williams syndrome, caused by haploinsufficiency of genes at 7q11.23. In such cases the loss-of-function acts as a dominant condition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gain-of-function involves inappropriate or excessive activity of a product, not a deficiency from losing one allele.
whyWrongByChoice.B: In a dominant-negative effect the abnormal product actively interferes with the normal protein; here one allele is simply deleted and no interfering product is made.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A silent mutation has no phenotypic effect, whereas this patient has a disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A conservative missense mutation is a single amino acid substitution, not a deletion causing reduced gene dosage.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A patient is heterozygous for a mutation in a gene encoding a protein that assembles into a multi-subunit complex. Although one allele is normal, the abnormal subunit not only fails to function but also binds and disrupts the normal subunits, so overall protein function is well below the 50% expected. Which of the following best describes this mechanism?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Simple recessive loss-of-function with a normal phenotype

explanation.summary: When an abnormal product interferes with the normal protein in a heterozygote, driving function below 50%, it is a dominant-negative effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The dominant-negative (double-negative) effect occurs when the abnormal gene product not only fails to function but actively interferes with the normal form produced by the other allele. Even though at least 50% functionality would be expected, function ends up much lower because the mutant protein disrupts the normal one, acting as if both alleles are defective.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In haploinsufficiency the normal allele simply cannot make enough product; the mutant product does not actively interfere with the normal protein.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A silent mutation causes no change in protein or function, contradicting the reduced function described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The mutation reduces function rather than conferring a new beneficial trait, so it is not a gain-of-function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A simple recessive loss-of-function in a heterozygote usually yields a normal phenotype, but here function drops well below 50%.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A tumor is found to carry a mutation that causes a gene's normal product to be expressed at abnormally high levels and at the wrong time in the cell cycle, driving unchecked proliferation. Which of the following best classifies this type of mutation?

explanation.summary: A mutation causing inappropriate, excessive, or mistimed expression (or a new abnormal activity) of a gene product is a gain-of-function mutation.
explanation.whyCorrect: A gain-of-function mutation causes the normal gene product to be expressed inappropriately, such as at abnormally high levels, at the wrong time, or in the wrong location, or gives the protein a new abnormal function. Excessive, mistimed expression that drives proliferation fits this definition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A loss-of-function mutation reduces gene activity, the opposite of the increased expression described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A null mutation causes the complete absence of a functional product, not overexpression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Haploinsufficiency is a deficiency in which 50% of product is not enough, not an increase in expression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A silent mutation causes no change in the product or its expression.

questions.topicName: DNA Point Mutations

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought in for a photosensitive rash over sun-exposed skin, intermittent cerebellar ataxia, and diarrhea. Urine amino acid analysis shows markedly elevated excretion of several amino acids, and the child improves with a niacin supplement. A defect in a renal transporter for neutral amino acids is suspected. Which of the following amino acids would be most expected to be increased in this patient's urine?

explanation.summary: Hartnup disease impairs reabsorption of neutral (uncharged) amino acids, so they are lost in the urine.
explanation.whyCorrect: The renal transporter defect in Hartnup disease causes loss of neutral amino acids, producing hyperaminoaciduria. Tryptophan is a neutral amino acid, and its depletion reduces NAD+ synthesis, producing a pellagra-like picture (diarrhea, dermatitis, dementia) that responds to niacin.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glutamate carries a negatively charged carboxylic acid side chain, so it is not among the neutral amino acids lost by this transporter.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspartate is acidic and negatively charged at blood pH, not neutral, so it is not preferentially wasted here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lysine has a basic, positively charged amine side chain and is not a neutral amino acid.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Arginine is basic and positively charged, so it is not handled by the neutral amino acid transporter.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: During the Bohr effect, a drop in tissue pH protonates a specific histidine residue in hemoglobin, stabilizing a lower-affinity conformation that favors oxygen unloading. Once protonated and positively charged, this histidine most logically forms a stabilizing ionic salt bridge with which of the following amino acids?

explanation.summary: A positively charged residue forms a salt bridge with a negatively charged partner.
explanation.whyCorrect: When histidine is protonated it becomes positively charged. An ionic salt bridge requires an oppositely charged partner, and the only amino acids with negatively charged side chains are aspartate and glutamate. Aspartate is therefore the correct partner.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Lysine is positively charged, so it would repel the protonated (positive) histidine rather than form a salt bridge.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Arginine is also positively charged and would electrostatically repel the protonated histidine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A second histidine would carry the same positive charge and could not form a stabilizing ionic bond.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylalanine has a nonpolar, uncharged side chain and cannot participate in an ionic salt bridge.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A signaling protein normally promotes cell division only after a hormone triggers its phosphorylation. In a certain cancer, a mutation replaces the modifiable residue with a different amino acid that mimics the phosphate group, causing constitutive activation independent of the hormone. Which single amino acid substitution best explains this constitutive, unregulated growth?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A threonine is replaced by an aspartate

choices.label: B
choices.text: A serine is replaced by a lysine

choices.label: C
choices.text: A lysine is replaced by a serine

choices.label: D
choices.text: An aspartate is replaced by a threonine

choices.label: E
choices.text: A leucine is replaced by an arginine

explanation.summary: A phosphomimetic swaps a phosphorylatable hydroxyl residue for a negatively charged residue that imitates the phosphate.
explanation.whyCorrect: The original residue must be serine, threonine, or tyrosine because only these hydroxyl-bearing residues are phosphorylated. Replacing threonine with aspartate introduces a permanent negative charge that mimics the phosphate group, locking the protein in its active state independent of the hormone.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Serine can be phosphorylated, but lysine adds a positive charge, which does not mimic the negative phosphate group.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This removes the phosphorylatable serine rather than mimicking phosphorylation, so it would not drive constitutive activity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspartate is not a phosphorylation target and threonine does not carry a negative charge, so this reverses the needed logic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neither leucine nor arginine can be phosphorylated, and arginine is positively charged, not phosphomimetic.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a diet lacking fresh fruits and vegetables presents with bleeding, inflamed gums, perifollicular hemorrhages, easy bruising, and poorly healing wounds. His fibroblasts contain a normally folded prolyl hydroxylase enzyme, yet collagen in his tissues lacks structural integrity. Which of the following best explains the defective collagen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deficiency of vitamin C, a required cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase

choices.label: B
choices.text: A missense mutation that misfolds and denatures prolyl hydroxylase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deficiency of vitamin B12 needed for peptide bond formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excess ubiquitination targeting collagen for proteasomal degradation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Failure of N-linked glycosylation of collagen asparagine residues

explanation.summary: Scurvy results from vitamin C deficiency, which deprives prolyl hydroxylase of its required cofactor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prolyl hydroxylase requires vitamin C (ascorbic acid) to hydroxylate proline to hydroxyproline, a modification that stabilizes collagen. In scurvy the enzyme is present and properly folded but simply lacks the cofactor, so collagen loses structural integrity and produces bleeding gums, perifollicular hemorrhage, and poor wound healing.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The vignette states the enzyme is normally folded; the problem is a missing nonprotein cofactor, not a denatured enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vitamin B12 is not the cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase, and peptide bonds are formed during translation, not by this enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The defect is failed hydroxylation, not excessive ubiquitin-tagged degradation of collagen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Collagen's key modification here is proline hydroxylation; N-linked glycosylation of asparagine is not the failed step in scurvy.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A cell biologist is studying N-linked glycosylation, in which a carbohydrate chain is covalently attached to a nitrogen atom of a specific amino acid side chain. Which amino acid is the target for N-linked glycosylation?

explanation.summary: N-linked glycosylation attaches sugar to the nitrogen of asparagine.
explanation.whyCorrect: N-linked glycosylation requires a nitrogen atom for the carbohydrate linkage, and asparagine is the only amino acid targeted for this modification. This routing modification is medically relevant because its disruption underlies I-cell disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Serine bears a hydroxyl group and is a target for O-linked glycosylation, not N-linked.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Threonine has a hydroxyl side chain used in O-linked glycosylation, not the nitrogen-based N-linkage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tyrosine's hydroxyl group supports O-linked glycosylation, not N-linked attachment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lysine's amine is the target for ubiquitination, not for N-linked glycosylation of proteins.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A damaged cytosolic protein is tagged for degradation when a small 76-amino-acid protein is covalently attached, and additional copies are then added to one another to form a polymeric tag. To which amino acid side chain is this tag initially attached?

explanation.summary: Ubiquitin is attached to the epsilon-amino group of a lysine side chain.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ubiquitin, a conserved 76-amino-acid protein, is covalently linked to the epsilon-amino group within a lysine side chain, and subsequent ubiquitin molecules are added to form a polyubiquitin chain that traffics the protein to the proteasome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Serine's hydroxyl group is a target for phosphorylation and O-linked glycosylation, not ubiquitin attachment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cysteine's thiol forms disulfide bonds; it is not the residue that anchors ubiquitin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspartate is a negatively charged residue and is not the site of ubiquitin conjugation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Proline can be hydroxylated in collagen but is not the anchor for ubiquitination.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A patient with multiple myeloma, a cancer of antibody-producing B cells, is treated with bortezomib, a proteasome inhibitor. These malignant cells rapidly ubiquitinate and degrade pro-apoptotic proteins to escape programmed cell death. Which of the following best explains how proteasome inhibition helps kill these cancer cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It blocks degradation of pro-apoptotic proteins, allowing them to accumulate and trigger apoptosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: It prevents lysosomal acidification, halting autophagy of mitochondria

choices.label: C
choices.text: It inhibits phosphorylation of serine residues needed for cell division

choices.label: D
choices.text: It chelates the calcium cofactor required for the coagulation cascade

choices.label: E
choices.text: It denatures the primary structure of antibodies by hydrolyzing peptide bonds

explanation.summary: Inhibiting the proteasome restores pro-apoptotic proteins that the cancer cells were destroying.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ubiquitin-proteasome pathway degrades polyubiquitin-tagged cytosolic proteins. Myeloma cells escape apoptosis by degrading pro-apoptotic proteins, so inhibiting the proteasome allows those proteins to accumulate, restoring apoptosis and enhancing cancer cell death.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bortezomib acts on the proteasome, not on lysosomal acidification or autophagy of organelles.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The drug blocks proteasomal degradation; it does not inhibit serine phosphorylation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Calcium chelation to block coagulation is the action of agents like EDTA, not a proteasome inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Peptide-bond hydrolysis of antibodies is not the mechanism; the drug prevents degradation of intracellular proteins.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A researcher wants to selectively destroy the primary structure of a protein without relying on disruption of weaker stabilizing forces. Which of the following agents or conditions is required to accomplish this?

explanation.summary: Primary structure is held by covalent peptide bonds, which only an enzyme can hydrolyze.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary structure is stabilized by covalent peptide bonds. Unlike the weaker forces that maintain higher-order structure, these bonds are not easily broken and require a protease, a hydrolytic enzyme, to cleave them with water.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Changes in pH neutralize charged side chains and disrupt ionic salt bridges, affecting higher-order structure but not covalent peptide bonds.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heat compromises weaker interactions such as hydrogen bonds while the covalent peptide backbone persists.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mechanical stress disrupts weak forces and promotes misfolding but does not cleave peptide bonds.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Detergents disrupt hydrophobic interactions and denature proteins without breaking the covalent primary structure.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A student is reviewing the forces that stabilize each level of protein structure. The two predominant elements of secondary structure, the alpha-helix and the beta-pleated sheet, are held together primarily by which of the following?

explanation.summary: Secondary structure (alpha-helix, beta-sheet) is stabilized by hydrogen bonds.
explanation.whyCorrect: The alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet are stabilized by hydrogen bonds, which are specialized dipole-dipole interactions occurring when hydrogen is attached to an electronegative atom such as oxygen, nitrogen, or sulfur.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Covalent peptide bonds stabilize primary structure, not the folding of secondary elements.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Disulfide bonds form between oxidized cysteines and help stabilize tertiary structure, not secondary structure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ionic salt bridges between charged side chains contribute to tertiary structure, not the backbone hydrogen bonding of secondary structure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Van der Waals interactions arise from transient dipoles among side chains and stabilize tertiary structure.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: Before running a coagulation-independent laboratory analysis, blood is collected into a vacutainer tube pre-loaded with EDTA. Despite the glass and plastic surfaces that normally activate clotting, the sample does not coagulate. Which of the following best explains why EDTA prevents coagulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It chelates Ca++, an inorganic cofactor required by coagulation proteins

choices.label: B
choices.text: It denatures the primary structure of clotting factors by hydrolysis

choices.label: C
choices.text: It phosphorylates serine residues on clotting factors to inhibit them

choices.label: D
choices.text: It donates NADH as a coenzyme to reverse the coagulation cascade

choices.label: E
choices.text: It covalently attaches a heme prosthetic group to fibrinogen

explanation.summary: EDTA sequesters calcium, a cofactor multiple coagulation proteins require.
explanation.whyCorrect: Multiple proteins in the coagulation cascade require the cofactor Ca++. EDTA is a metal chelator that binds and coordinates positively charged metals like Ca++, sequestering them away from the clotting proteins so the cascade cannot proceed. The proteins themselves remain intact but lack their required cofactor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: EDTA does not hydrolyze peptide bonds or denature the primary structure of clotting factors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: EDTA chelates metal ions; it does not phosphorylate residues.
whyWrongByChoice.D: NADH is an intracellular coenzyme and is unrelated to EDTA's chelating mechanism in the tube.
whyWrongByChoice.E: EDTA sequesters calcium rather than adding a heme prosthetic group to any protein.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: An examiner asks a student to classify the non-protein components proteins require for function. Heme, which is covalently linked within a hemoglobin subunit and whose removal disrupts the protein's structure and function, is best described as which of the following?

explanation.summary: A cofactor covalently linked to a protein is a prosthetic group.
explanation.whyCorrect: A prosthetic group is a cofactor that is covalently linked to the protein structure, with heme given as the classic example. Unlike loosely bound cofactors, removal of a prosthetic group often disrupts structure and function and cannot simply be restored by adding it back.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Inorganic cofactors such as metal ions are typically loosely associated and readily removable, unlike a covalently attached heme.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A coenzyme is an organic cofactor such as NADH or FADH2 and is not defined by covalent attachment to the protein.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A substrate is the molecule an enzyme acts upon, not a bound non-protein helper.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An apoenzyme is the protein portion lacking its cofactor, not the covalently bound helper itself.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A key step in influenza infection is the ability of a latent viral fusion protein to undergo a conformational change that promotes viral spread. This change occurs after the extracellular virus is internalized by the cell and the fusion protein is exposed to a slightly acidic environment. Which of the following best describes a vital aspect of this process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A histidine on the fusion protein becomes protonated and positively charged within the lysosome

choices.label: B
choices.text: A histidine on the fusion protein becomes deprotonated and negatively charged within the lysosome

choices.label: C
choices.text: A histidine on the fusion protein becomes protonated and positively charged within the proteasome

choices.label: D
choices.text: A histidine on the fusion protein becomes deprotonated and negatively charged within the proteasome

choices.label: E
choices.text: A serine on the fusion protein becomes phosphorylated within the proteasome

explanation.summary: An internalized protein is routed to the acidic lysosome, where histidine becomes protonated and positively charged.
explanation.whyCorrect: Extracellular material that is internalized is routed to the lysosome, which has an acidic interior (pH 4.5-5.0). Because histidine has a side-chain pKa near physiological pH, this acidic environment protonates it, giving it a positive charge that drives the fusion protein's conformational change.

whyWrongByChoice.B: In an acidic environment histidine gains a proton and becomes positively charged, not deprotonated and negative.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The proteasome degrades cytosolic proteins and is not the acidic compartment that receives internalized extracellular virus.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The proteasome is the wrong compartment, and acidic conditions would protonate rather than deprotonate histidine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serine phosphorylation is a signaling modification and does not describe the acid-driven conformational change of the fusion protein.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I

questions.stem: A newborn develops a hemolytic crisis in the first days of life and is found to have glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency. On a later rotation, an otherwise healthy 53-year-old man is diagnosed with the exact same enzyme deficiency, yet this is his first-ever hemolytic episode. Which of the following best explains the difference in age at initial presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The older adult acquired the deficiency later in life rather than inheriting it

choices.label: B
choices.text: G6PD enzyme activity naturally increases with age, protecting older patients

choices.label: C
choices.text: The two patients carry different G6PD variants that differ in residual enzyme activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: The adult's red blood cells contain mitochondria that compensate for the defect

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced glutathione is only required in neonatal red blood cells

explanation.summary: G6PD deficiency is a spectrum disorder whose severity depends on which of the many variants a patient carries.
explanation.whyCorrect: Over 200 pathogenic G6PD variants (and 400+ variant enzymes) are described, and the disease occurs along a spectrum of severity. Some variants cause hemolysis within days of birth, while others may never cause a crisis unless the right oxidative stressor is introduced. The age of presentation is entirely dependent on the specific mutation and how it impacts enzyme function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: G6PD deficiency is an inherited X-linked disorder present from birth, not something acquired in adulthood.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Enzyme activity does not rise with age; residual activity is set by the inherited variant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mature red blood cells lack mitochondria entirely, which is precisely why they depend on pentose-phosphate-pathway NADPH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reduced glutathione is required throughout life in all red blood cells, not only in neonates.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: An otherwise healthy 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with abnormally dark urine after starting a sulfa drug for a urinary tract infection. Blood work shows a low hematocrit with an increased reticulocyte count, indicating hemolytic anemia. This patient's condition is most likely due to failure of which of the following functions?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Production of the amino acid cysteine

explanation.summary: G6PD deficiency prevents NADPH production, so red blood cells cannot regenerate reduced glutathione to neutralize oxidative agents.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sulfa drug is a classic oxidative stressor that triggers hemolysis in G6PD deficiency. Without G6PD, red cells cannot make NADPH from the pentose phosphate pathway, so they cannot reduce glutathione and neutralize accumulating oxidizing species, leading to red-cell destruction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Defective elastin formation is the problem in Marfan syndrome, not a cause of hemolytic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Oxidative phosphorylation would be reduced by anemia, but a decrease in ATP production is not what causes the hemolysis here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Failure to degrade lysosomal cargo describes lysosomal storage disorders such as Hurler or I-cell disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Impaired cysteine production reflects cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency (homocystinuria), not G6PD deficiency.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy of Mediterranean descent develops jaundice and dark urine one day after eating fava beans. A peripheral blood smear shows red cells with membrane "bites" removed and inclusions of denatured hemoglobin. Which of the following enzymes is most likely deficient?

explanation.summary: Favism with Heinz bodies and bite cells is the classic picture of G6PD deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fava beans are a well-known oxidative trigger in G6PD deficiency, which is common in individuals of Mediterranean heritage. The inclusions of oxidatively denatured hemoglobin are Heinz bodies, and the bite cells form when macrophages remove those Heinz-body regions from the red cell membrane.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-L-iduronidase deficiency causes Hurler syndrome, a lysosomal storage disorder, not acute hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency causes homocystinuria, presenting with thrombosis and lens dislocation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency causes PKU, presenting with a mousy odor and developmental delay.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fibrillin-1 is defective in Marfan syndrome, a connective tissue disorder.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A newborn is noted to have a musty, mousy body odor. Over the following months the infant develops progressive developmental delay and unusually fair skin and hair. Which of the following enzymatic conversions is most likely impaired?

explanation.summary: Classic PKU results from deficient phenylalanine hydroxylase, which normally converts phenylalanine to tyrosine.
explanation.whyCorrect: In PKU, phenylalanine hydroxylase cannot convert phenylalanine to tyrosine, so phenylalanine accumulates to neurotoxic levels (causing developmental delay) and generates metabolites that produce a mousy odor. Because melanin synthesis requires tyrosine, affected patients are typically fair-complected.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Impaired conversion of homocysteine to cysteine occurs in homocystinuria, which does not cause a mousy odor or fair skin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Failure to degrade glycosaminoglycans describes Hurler syndrome, a lysosomal storage disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Failure to regenerate reduced glutathione describes G6PD deficiency and its hemolytic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Loss of neutrophil elastase inhibition describes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency and its lung disease.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A child with a milder form of phenylketonuria (PKU) shows partial clinical improvement when supplemented with a specific organic cofactor used by phenylalanine hydroxylase. Which of the following is this cofactor?

explanation.summary: Phenylalanine hydroxylase uses tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), and some milder PKU variants respond to BH4 supplementation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylalanine hydroxylase uses the GTP-derived organic cofactor tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) to hydroxylate phenylalanine to tyrosine. When a variant enzyme binds its cofactor poorly, raising cofactor concentration can drive binding and partially restore function, which is why some milder PKU forms are BH4-responsive.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pyridoxal phosphate (vitamin B6) is the cofactor for cystathionine beta-synthase in homocystinuria, not for phenylalanine hydroxylase.
whyWrongByChoice.B: FAD (from vitamin B2) serves glutathione reductase, not phenylalanine hydroxylase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cobalamin (B12) participates in homocysteine remethylation, not phenylalanine hydroxylation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NADPH is produced by the pentose phosphate pathway and used to reduce glutathione, not by phenylalanine hydroxylase.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 14-month-old boy has coarse facial features, joint stiffness, developmental delay, and increased urinary mucopolysaccharides. Enzyme testing reveals deficient alpha-L-iduronidase, confirming Hurler syndrome. Which of the following features would best distinguish this disorder from Hunter syndrome?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accumulation of heparan and dermatan sulfate

explanation.summary: Corneal clouding is present in Hurler but absent in Hunter syndrome, making it a key distinguishing feature.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hurler and Hunter syndromes both accumulate heparan and dermatan sulfate and share many features, but Hunter syndrome is distinct in that there is no corneal clouding, it is X-linked, it includes aggressive behavior, and it is due to iduronate sulfatase deficiency. The presence of corneal clouding therefore points to Hurler.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Accumulation of heparan and dermatan sulfate is shared by both Hurler and Hunter syndromes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coarse facial features occur in both disorders and cannot distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Both disorders result from deficiency of a lysosomal enzyme, so this is not distinguishing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Progressive developmental delay is a shared manifestation of both syndromes.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: An infant has severe developmental delay, coarse facial features, and failure to thrive. Cultured fibroblasts contain dense intracellular inclusion bodies. Laboratory testing shows markedly ELEVATED lysosomal hydrolase levels in the plasma with normal urinary mucopolysaccharides. Which of the following processes is defective?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Addition of the mannose-6-phosphate tag that targets enzymes to the lysosome

explanation.summary: I-cell disease results from defective GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase, which fails to add mannose-6-phosphate, so lysosomal enzymes are secreted instead of delivered to lysosomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: In I-cell disease, GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase cannot form mannose-6-phosphate on lysosomal enzymes in the Golgi. Without this tag, trans-Golgi receptors cannot route the hydrolases to lysosomes, so the enzymes spill into the circulation (elevated plasma levels) while undegraded material accumulates as dense inclusion bodies. Urinary mucopolysaccharides are not increased, unlike in Hurler syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Deficient alpha-L-iduronidase causes Hurler syndrome, which raises urinary mucopolysaccharides and affects only one enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Defective phenylalanine hydroxylation causes PKU, not a lysosomal trafficking disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Failure to reduce glutathione causes G6PD-related hemolytic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Defective tropoelastin multimerization reflects fibrillin-1 dysfunction in Marfan syndrome.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A tall, thin 17-year-old has long fingers with positive thumb and wrist signs and is found to have upward-and-outward dislocation of the ocular lens along with aortic root dilation. Which of the following genes is most likely mutated?

explanation.summary: Marfan syndrome arises from mutation of the fibrillin-1 gene, FBN1.
explanation.whyCorrect: The tall stature, arachnodactyly (positive thumb and wrist signs), upward-and-outward lens dislocation, and aortic root dilation are classic for Marfan syndrome. Marfan arises from an autosomal dominant mutation of FBN1, which encodes fibrillin-1, the scaffold protein for elastin in connective tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SERPINA1 mutations cause alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency with lung and liver disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PAH mutations cause PKU.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IDUA mutations cause Hurler syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: G6PD mutations cause hemolytic anemia, not a connective tissue phenotype.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: In Marfan syndrome, mutation of fibrillin-1 produces a defective elastin scaffold in connective tissue. Which of the following secondary molecular changes is chiefly responsible for the aortic pathology seen in these patients?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased transforming growth factor beta (TGF-beta) activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of mannose-6-phosphate tagging of enzymes

explanation.summary: In Marfan syndrome, FBN1 mutation secondarily increases TGF-beta activity, which drives the aortic pathology.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beyond producing defective elastin, the FBN1 mutation secondarily increases the levels and activity of transforming growth factor beta (TGF-beta), a signaling protein. Abnormal TGF-beta activation is what primarily drives the aortic dissection and aneurysm risk associated with Marfan syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Accumulation of homocysteine is the mechanism in homocystinuria, a biochemically distinct disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Oxidative denaturation of hemoglobin occurs in G6PD deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Loss of mannose-6-phosphate tagging is the defect in I-cell disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Unopposed neutrophil elastase activity underlies alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl is brought in for a decline in her speaking ability. She has intellectual delay, long thin extremities, and vision problems from a downward-and-inward dislocation of the ocular lens. Which of the following proteins is most likely defective?

explanation.summary: Homocystinuria, most commonly from cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency, presents with intellectual disability, marfanoid habitus, and downward-and-inward lens dislocation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of intellectual disability, marfanoid long limbs, and a down-and-in (inferonasal) lens dislocation is characteristic of homocystinuria. The most common enzyme deficiency causing homocystinuria is cystathionine beta-synthase, which normally consumes homocysteine to make cysteine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency causes hemolytic anemia, not lens dislocation and intellectual disability.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibrillin-1 defects cause Marfan syndrome, in which the lens dislocates up and out and intellect is preserved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alpha-L-iduronidase deficiency causes Hurler syndrome, presenting in infancy with coarse features and corneal clouding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency causes PKU, which does not feature lens dislocation.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: Marfan syndrome and homocystinuria share several clinical features despite having unrelated biochemical causes. Which of the following findings points specifically to homocystinuria rather than Marfan syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intellectual disability with a high risk of thrombosis

explanation.summary: Intellectual disability and thrombotic risk are specific to homocystinuria, whereas long limbs, vision problems, and cardiovascular events are shared with Marfan syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Marfan syndrome and homocystinuria share long appendages, impaired vision, and adverse cardiovascular events. Homocystinuria is distinguished by intellectual disability (when untreated) and a high risk of thrombosis leading to early myocardial infarction or stroke, features not seen in Marfan syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Long limbs and appendages (marfanoid habitus) are a shared feature of both disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Impaired vision from lens dislocation occurs in both, differing only in the direction of dislocation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adverse cardiovascular events occur in both conditions and therefore do not distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autosomal dominant inheritance describes Marfan syndrome, not homocystinuria.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a minimal smoking history has early-onset emphysema affecting the lower lobes of the lungs. He is also found to have cirrhosis with elevated liver enzymes. Genetic testing reveals that he is homozygous for the Z allele (Glu342Lys) of the alpha-1 antitrypsin gene. Which of the following best explains his liver involvement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neutrophil elastase digests the hepatic extracellular matrix

choices.label: B
choices.text: Homocysteine accumulation causes hepatic vein thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Misfolded AAT accumulates in the hepatocyte endoplasmic reticulum, causing cell stress and death

choices.label: E
choices.text: Complete absence of AAT protein production

explanation.summary: The Z allele produces a misfolded alpha-1 antitrypsin that accumulates in the hepatocyte ER, causing cellular stress and liver disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Z allele replaces a glutamate with lysine (Glu342Lys), a nonconservative change that causes AAT to misfold within the liver endoplasmic reticulum. This misfolded protein induces cellular stress and hepatocyte death, producing cirrhosis, elevated liver enzymes, decreased protein production, and increased hepatic cancer risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neutrophil elastase drives the lung disease by degrading the alveolar extracellular matrix, not the liver pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Homocysteine-related thrombosis is a feature of homocystinuria, not AAT deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glycosaminoglycan accumulation in lysosomes describes Hurler syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rarer null variants make minimal or no AAT and, lacking misfolding stress, spare the liver while still causing lung disease; this patient's Z allele does produce protein that misfolds.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders I (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A researcher measures the equilibrium constant of a reversible reaction with and without a purified enzyme present. The enzyme dramatically increases the rate at which equilibrium is reached. Which of the following is the enzyme most likely to leave unchanged?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The rate of the forward reaction

choices.label: B
choices.text: The activation energy required for the reaction

choices.label: C
choices.text: The overall equilibrium (thermodynamic spontaneity) of the reaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: The rate at which the transition state is formed

choices.label: E
choices.text: The time required for product to accumulate

explanation.summary: Enzymes accelerate reaction rates (kinetics) but do not alter thermodynamics.
explanation.whyCorrect: Enzymes are catalysts that lower the activation energy and speed the rate at which a reaction proceeds, but they do not change whether the reaction is thermodynamically favorable or shift its equilibrium position. A catalyzed reaction reaches the same equilibrium as an uncatalyzed one, only faster.

whyWrongByChoice.A: By lowering activation energy the enzyme increases the forward rate, so this is changed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lowering the activation energy is the defining action of an enzyme, so this is changed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Enzymes favor formation of the transition state, accelerating its appearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Because the rate is increased, product accumulates in less time.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: An otherwise healthy patient develops fasting hypoglycemia after weeks of eating raw eggs, which sequester biotin. The impaired reaction adds CO2 to pyruvate to form oxaloacetate in an ATP-dependent step of gluconeogenesis. Which class of enzyme catalyzes this diminished reaction?

explanation.summary: A biotin-dependent carboxylase that covalently joins two substrates using ATP is a ligase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ligases form a covalent bond between substrates in an ATP-dependent manner; the carboxylase subclass adds CO2 to a substrate to form a carboxylic acid and requires biotin (vitamin B7). Pyruvate carboxylase adding CO2 to pyruvate fits this exactly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No oxidation-reduction (electron transfer) occurs in this reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A lyase breaks bonds without hydrolysis or oxidation; here a bond is being formed with ATP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The molecular formula changes between substrates and product, so this is not a simple isomerization.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No functional group is being swapped between two substrates; a new covalent bond is being created.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: During glycogenolysis in skeletal muscle, an enzyme uses free inorganic phosphate (Pi) to cleave a glucose unit from glycogen, releasing glucose-1-phosphate. Which term best describes this enzyme?

explanation.summary: A phosphorylase uses inorganic phosphate to break a bond and generate a phosphorylated product.
explanation.whyCorrect: A phosphorylase adds phosphate onto a substrate but, unlike a kinase, it uses free inorganic phosphate (Pi) rather than ATP to break bonds. Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves glycogen with Pi to liberate glucose-1-phosphate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A kinase transfers a phosphate from ATP, not from free inorganic phosphate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A phosphatase removes a phosphate using water; here phosphate is being added.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phosphoglucomutase relocates a phosphate on the sugar (glucose-1-P to glucose-6-P) rather than cleaving glycogen with Pi.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An isomerase rearranges a substrate without adding phosphate.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A 20-year-old athlete reports painful muscle cramps that occur only during intense exercise. Muscle biopsy shows abnormal glycogen accumulation in skeletal muscle, and testing reveals deficiency of the muscle isoform of glycogen phosphorylase. Which of the following best explains the exertional symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Muscle cannot liberate glucose substrates from glycogen for ATP production during exertion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Muscle glycogen synthesis is blocked, depleting glycogen stores

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lysosomal enzymes are mistrafficked out of the cell

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ammonia accumulates because transamination is impaired

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pyruvate kinase deficiency causes red-cell hemolysis

explanation.summary: McArdle disease (type V glycogen storage disease) results from muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Deficiency of the muscle isoform of glycogen phosphorylase (McArdle disease) prevents breakdown of glycogen, so glycogen accumulates and engorges the muscle. During exertion the muscle cannot provide sufficient glucose-derived substrate for ATP production, producing painful cramps.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The defect is in glycogen breakdown, not synthesis; glycogen accumulates rather than depletes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mistrafficking of lysosomal enzymes describes I-cell disease, a defect of post-translational mannose-6-phosphate tagging.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ammonia buildup relates to impaired transamination, not glycogen phosphorylase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyruvate kinase deficiency affects red blood cells and causes hemolytic anemia, not exertional muscle cramps.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A patient develops jaundice, fatigue, and pallor after eating fava beans and is found to have an underlying red-cell enzyme deficiency causing acute hemolysis. Which plasma biomarker is most likely to be elevated as a result of the red-cell destruction?

explanation.summary: LDH is highly enriched in red blood cells, so hemolysis releases it into plasma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lactate dehydrogenase is present in many tissues but is highly enriched in red blood cells, which depend on interconversion of lactate and pyruvate. When RBCs lyse (as in G6PD deficiency triggered by fava beans), circulating LDH rises.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PSA is a marker of prostate tissue, not red-cell turnover.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lipase indicates pancreatic pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lysosomal enzymes in the circulation point to I-cell disease, not hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GGT is a marker of hepatobiliary or pancreatic membranes, not red-cell lysis.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with acute crushing chest pain. The clinician wants an enzyme biomarker that is relatively specific for cardiac muscle injury. Which of the following is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: CK-MB isoforms are a biomarker of cardiac (heart) injury such as myocardial infarction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Creatine kinase-MB isoforms serve as a biomarker of the heart, and their rise in plasma indicates myocardial injury such as a myocardial infarction, matching this presentation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ALT is a marker of liver injury.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ALP reflects liver or bone disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pancreatic alpha-amylase points to pancreatic pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GGT indicates hepatobiliary (and sometimes pancreatic) disease, not myocardial injury.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: Two isozymes, A and B, act on the same substrate and have nearly identical Vmax values (~200 umol/L/s). Isozyme A reaches half of Vmax at a substrate concentration of 10 mM, whereas isozyme B reaches half of Vmax at 5 mM. Which isozyme has the higher affinity for the substrate, and why?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isozyme A, because it has the smaller Km

choices.label: B
choices.text: Isozyme A, because it has the larger Km

choices.label: C
choices.text: Isozyme B, because it has the smaller Km

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isozyme B, because it has the larger Km

choices.label: E
choices.text: They have equal affinity because their Vmax values are equal

explanation.summary: Km is the substrate concentration at half of Vmax, and a lower Km means higher affinity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Km equals the substrate concentration when velocity is one-half of Vmax. Isozyme B reaches half-Vmax at a lower substrate concentration (5 mM vs 10 mM), so it has the smaller Km. Because Km and affinity are inversely related, the smaller Km reflects higher affinity for the substrate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Isozyme A has the larger Km, not the smaller one, so this reasoning is wrong.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A larger Km indicates lower, not higher, affinity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Isozyme B has the smaller Km, not the larger one.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Affinity is determined by Km, not by Vmax, so equal Vmax values do not imply equal affinity.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A student assumes that raising the substrate concentration to 10 times an enzyme's Km will drive a Michaelis-Menten reaction all the way to Vmax. For an enzyme obeying Michaelis-Menten kinetics, what velocity is actually achieved when [S] = 10 x Km?

explanation.summary: Because Vmax is an asymptote, even [S] = 10 x Km reaches only about 91% of Vmax.
explanation.whyCorrect: Using V0 = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]) with [S] = 10Km gives V0 = (Vmax x 10Km)/(Km + 10Km) = 10Vmax/11 = 0.91 Vmax. Vmax is only a theoretical asymptote that the curve approaches, so high substrate concentrations never fully reach it.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This underestimates the velocity; at 10x Km the enzyme is well past the low-substrate range.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 0.33 Vmax would correspond to a substrate concentration well below Km.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 0.50 Vmax occurs when [S] equals Km, not 10 times Km.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vmax is an asymptote and is only truly reached at infinite substrate; it cannot be achieved at a finite concentration.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: An investigator plots reaction velocity against substrate concentration for a multi-subunit enzyme and obtains a sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve rather than a hyperbolic one. Which property best explains why this enzyme does not obey Michaelis-Menten kinetics?

explanation.summary: Michaelis-Menten kinetics apply only to enzymes that lack cooperativity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Michaelis-Menten kinetics only apply to enzymes that lack cooperativity. Multi-subunit enzymes with quaternary structure can show allostery between subunits, generating a sigmoidal curve (like hemoglobin's oxygen binding), whereas a non-cooperative monomer (like myoglobin) gives a hyperbolic curve.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Phosphorylation is a regulatory modification but is not what makes the velocity curve sigmoidal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A high turnover number affects how fast product forms, not the shape of the curve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Product inhibition alters apparent kinetics but does not itself produce a sigmoidal cooperativity curve.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Denaturation would reduce activity rather than create a cooperative S-shaped curve.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A pharmacologist uses a Lineweaver-Burk (double-reciprocal) plot to characterize how a drug alters enzyme kinetics. On this plot, which parameter is represented by the x-intercept?

explanation.summary: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot the x-intercept equals the negative reciprocal of Km.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Lineweaver-Burk plot is a double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten plot. On it, the x-intercept equals -1/Km, the y-intercept equals 1/Vmax, and the slope equals Km/Vmax. This makes changes in Km and Vmax easier to detect when studying drugs that alter kinetics.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 1/Vmax is the y-intercept, not the x-intercept.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vmax itself is not read directly off an intercept; it is the reciprocal of the y-intercept.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Km/Vmax is the slope of the line, not an intercept.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Km is obtained as the negative reciprocal of the x-intercept, not the x-intercept value itself.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: An enzyme's active site is lined with several aspartate and glutamate residues. Which of the following is the most likely chemical feature of its preferred substrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A region composed of several basic (positively charged) amino acid residues

choices.label: B
choices.text: A region composed of several acidic (negatively charged) amino acid residues

choices.label: C
choices.text: A region composed of several bulky, aromatic nonpolar residues

choices.label: D
choices.text: A region composed of several small, nonpolar residues

choices.label: E
choices.text: A hydrophobic core that excludes water entirely

explanation.summary: An active site rich in acidic residues favors binding a positively charged (basic) substrate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aspartate and glutamate have negatively charged, acidic side chains, so the active site is negatively charged. This favors recognition and binding of a positively charged substrate, such as one enriched in basic residues like lysine and arginine.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An acidic substrate would carry the same negative charge as the active site, causing repulsion instead of attraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bulky nonpolar residues would not favor association with the polar, negatively charged active site.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Small nonpolar residues also lack the positive charge needed to complement an acidic active site.
