whyWrongByChoice.E: The active site is polar and charged here, so a purely hydrophobic, water-excluding substrate would not bind well.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: After moderate drinking, blood ethanol reaches 10-20 mM, while alcohol dehydrogenase has a Km of about 1-2 mM for ethanol. Because [S] far exceeds Km, which factor is the rate-limiting determinant of ethanol clearance, and why does blood alcohol accumulate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Substrate availability, because the enzyme is far from saturation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enzyme turnover, because the enzyme is saturated and operating near Vmax

choices.label: C
choices.text: The activation energy, which rises as ethanol accumulates

choices.label: E
choices.text: The Km, which increases as more ethanol is consumed

explanation.summary: When [S] greatly exceeds Km the enzyme is saturated, so enzyme turnover becomes rate-limiting.
explanation.whyCorrect: When substrate concentration exceeds Km, enzyme turnover (how fast the enzyme can operate) is the rate-limiting factor. Because ethanol (10-20 mM) far exceeds the Km of alcohol dehydrogenase (1-2 mM), the enzyme is saturated and working near Vmax, so alcohol builds up in the blood faster than it can be cleared.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Substrate availability is rate-limiting only when [S] is below Km; here [S] is well above Km.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Activation energy is a fixed property lowered by the enzyme and does not rise with substrate accumulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The rate limitation here reflects saturation kinetics, not subunit cooperativity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Km is an intrinsic constant of the enzyme and does not increase simply because more ethanol is consumed.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A researcher compares two isozymes that both catalyze the first step of glycolysis. Enzyme X, found in red blood cells and neurons, becomes half-maximally active at a very low glucose concentration, whereas enzyme Y, found in hepatocytes, requires a much higher glucose concentration to reach half-maximal activity. Which property best explains why enzyme X can phosphorylate glucose even during fasting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enzyme X has a low Km, reflecting high affinity for glucose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enzyme X has a high Km, reflecting low affinity for glucose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enzyme X has a higher Vmax than enzyme Y

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enzyme X is only expressed when blood glucose is high

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enzyme X binds glucose at an allosteric site rather than the active site

explanation.summary: Hexokinase's low Km gives it high glucose affinity, letting glucose-dependent cells trap glucose even at low blood levels.
explanation.whyCorrect: A low Km means the enzyme reaches half-maximal velocity at a low substrate concentration, i.e., high affinity for glucose. Hexokinase (enzyme X) is expressed in neurons and RBCs, which rely on circulating glucose, so its low Km permits rapid conversion of glucose to glucose 6-phosphate even when blood glucose is low.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A high Km indicates low affinity and describes glucokinase (enzyme Y), which cannot phosphorylate glucose efficiently when levels are low.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hexokinase actually has a lower Vmax than glucokinase; this prevents overaccumulation rather than explaining low-glucose function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Being active only at high glucose describes the liver's glucokinase, not the RBC/neuron enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both hexokinase and glucokinase phosphorylate glucose at the active site, not an allosteric site.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: In the liver, glucokinase (hexokinase IV) has both a higher Km and a higher Vmax than the hexokinase found in most other tissues. During a period of glucose abundance after a large meal, what is the physiological advantage of this kinetic profile for hepatocytes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It allows the liver to trap glucose even when blood glucose is very low

choices.label: B
choices.text: It permits rapid, high-volume conversion of glucose to glucose 6-phosphate for storage pathways only when glucose is plentiful

choices.label: C
choices.text: It prevents the liver from ever forming glucose 6-phosphate

choices.label: D
choices.text: It ensures the liver competes with neurons for scarce glucose during fasting

choices.label: E
choices.text: It makes the liver independent of glucose concentration for its rate of glucose phosphorylation

explanation.summary: Glucokinase's high Km plus high Vmax lets the liver phosphorylate glucose rapidly, but only when glucose is abundant, feeding storage pathways.
explanation.whyCorrect: The high Km means glucokinase only becomes substantially active at elevated glucose (e.g., postprandial), which spares blood glucose during fasting. The high Vmax then allows fast, efficient accumulation of glucose 6-phosphate to supply anabolic storage pathways such as glycogen and lipid synthesis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Trapping glucose at very low concentrations describes low-Km hexokinase, not high-Km glucokinase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucokinase does form glucose 6-phosphate; it simply restricts this to high-glucose conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The high Km specifically spares blood glucose for other tissues during fasting rather than competing with neurons.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glucokinase activity is strongly dependent on glucose concentration; it rises steeply as glucose increases.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A pharmacologist studies a drug that binds reversibly and noncovalently to the active site of an enzyme and structurally resembles the natural substrate. On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, adding this drug shifts the x-intercept to be less negative but leaves the y-intercept unchanged. Which type of inhibitor is this drug?

explanation.summary: A reversible active-site inhibitor that resembles substrate, raises apparent Km, and leaves Vmax unchanged is competitive.
explanation.whyCorrect: Competitive inhibitors compete with substrate at the active site and commonly mimic its structure. On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, Vmax (y-intercept) is unchanged because excess substrate can still overcome the inhibitor, while the apparent Km rises, seen as a less negative x-intercept (-1/Km).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Noncompetitive inhibitors bind an allosteric site, leave Km unchanged, and decrease Vmax (increased y-intercept).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Uncompetitive inhibitors decrease both Km and Vmax and bind only after substrate is bound.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Irreversible/suicide inhibitors bind covalently, are not overcome by dilution, and decrease Vmax without changing Km.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An activator would increase activity, not inhibit; it would not raise apparent Km.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: An enzyme is exposed to an inhibitor that binds an allosteric site, does not resemble the substrate, and cannot be overcome by increasing substrate concentration. The enzyme-substrate complex still forms normally. Which pair of kinetic changes is expected?

explanation.summary: Noncompetitive inhibitors leave Km unchanged and decrease Vmax.
explanation.whyCorrect: A noncompetitive inhibitor binds an allosteric site, changes enzyme shape, and reduces product formation regardless of substrate concentration. Because it does not compete at the active site, the enzyme-substrate complex forms normally and Km is unchanged, but Vmax falls because functional catalysis is reduced.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased Km with unchanged Vmax describes a competitive inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Decreased Km and decreased Vmax describes an uncompetitive inhibitor, which binds only after substrate is bound.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No inhibitor increases Vmax; that would be activation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Unchanged Km and Vmax would mean no inhibitory effect at all.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A toxicology exam asks which single feature is shared by a reversible competitive inhibitor and an irreversible suicide inhibitor acting on the same enzyme. Which of the following is that shared feature?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both increase the apparent Km of the enzyme

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both bind at the active site and tend to resemble the substrate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both bind at an allosteric site

explanation.summary: Competitive and suicide inhibitors both act at the active site and resemble the substrate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both a competitive enzyme inhibitor and an irreversible suicide inhibitor bind the active site, and to do so they chemically resemble the substrate to some extent. Their key difference is the bond formed: competitive inhibitors bind noncovalently and reversibly, while suicide inhibitors bind covalently and irreversibly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Only the reversible competitive inhibitor is relieved by dilution; covalent suicide inhibition is not.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Only the competitive inhibitor raises apparent Km; the suicide inhibitor leaves Km unchanged.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neither binds an allosteric site; both act at the active site.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The competitive inhibitor leaves Vmax unchanged, but the suicide inhibitor decreases Vmax.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after ingesting an organophosphate pesticide. This class of poison, along with drugs such as allopurinol and aspirin, acts by binding covalently to an enzyme's active site so that the inhibition cannot be reversed by dilution. What is the expected effect on the enzyme system's kinetics?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vmax is lowered while the affinity (Km) of the remaining enzyme for substrate is unchanged

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vmax is unchanged while Km is increased

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both Km and Vmax are increased

choices.label: D
choices.text: Km is decreased and Vmax is unchanged

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enzyme function is fully restored by simply diluting the inhibitor

explanation.summary: Irreversible (suicide) inhibitors covalently remove functional enzyme, lowering Vmax without changing the Km of the remaining enzyme.
explanation.whyCorrect: Irreversible inhibitors bind covalently and effectively subtract functional enzyme molecules from the mixture, so Vmax drops. The enzyme copies that remain unbound behave normally, so their affinity for substrate (Km) is unchanged. Organophosphates, allopurinol, and aspirin are cited as suicide inhibitors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Unchanged Vmax with increased Km describes a reversible competitive inhibitor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increasing both Km and Vmax is not a recognized inhibition pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased Km with unchanged Vmax does not describe irreversible inhibition (it partially resembles uncompetitive inhibition).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Covalent irreversible inhibition is not removed by dilution; recovery requires synthesis of new enzyme.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: During a lecture on hepatic drug metabolism, phase I and phase II reactions are contrasted. Which statement most accurately distinguishes them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phase I reactions conjugate polar groups; phase II reactions oxidize substrates

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phase I reactions (largely CYP450-catalyzed) oxidize/hydrolyze substrates, while phase II reactions covalently add polar groups such as glucuronate, sulfate, or glutathione

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phase I and phase II reactions both must occur strictly in sequence and cannot occur independently

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phase II enzymes generate reactive oxygen species using NADPH, while phase I enzymes do not

choices.label: E
choices.text: Phase I reactions decrease water solubility, while phase II reactions decrease it further

explanation.summary: Phase I enzymes (CYP450) oxidize/hydrolyze xenobiotics; phase II enzymes conjugate polar groups to boost water solubility.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phase I reactions transform molecules chiefly by oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis, or hydroxylation and are largely catalyzed by CYP450 enzymes. Phase II reactions are conjugations in which polar molecules such as glutathione, glucuronate, or sulfate are covalently linked to the substrate, increasing water solubility for excretion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the two phases; oxidation is phase I and conjugation is phase II.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The brick states the pattern is generally sequential but that broad specificity allows phase II to occur apart from phase I in some cases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ROS generation and NADPH use are hallmarks of the CYP450 (phase I) enzymes, not phase II.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both phases increase water solubility to promote excretion, not decrease it.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A member of the CYP450 enzyme family is described as containing a heme prosthetic group, requiring NADPH as a coenzyme, being associated with ER membranes and mitochondria, and being inducible by substrate abundance. Which additional property is characteristic of all CYP450 enzymes and contributes to their potential cytotoxicity?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They generate a free-radical/reactive oxygen intermediate that can be cytotoxic upon accumulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: They bind substrate irreversibly and cannot be induced

explanation.summary: All CYP450 enzymes generate a free-radical/ROS intermediate that can be cytotoxic when it accumulates.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists that all CYP450 members oxidize substrate while reducing oxygen, require NADPH to produce a reduced active oxygen species that can form reactive oxygen species, and generate a free-radical intermediate that upon accumulation can be cytotoxic. This underlies liver damage from ethanol oxidation via CYP2E1.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glutathione conjugation is a phase II reaction performed by glutathione S-transferase, not CYP450.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucose phosphorylation is done by hexokinase/glucokinase, unrelated to CYP450.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucuronate addition to bilirubin is done by UDP-glucuronosyltransferase, a phase II enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP450 enzymes are inducible by substrate abundance, so this statement is incorrect.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with heavy chronic alcohol use is being counseled about medication safety. He notes that at high levels of alcohol intake, a larger proportion of ingested ethanol is handled by CYP2E1 rather than alcohol dehydrogenase. Which statement about CYP2E1 best explains this shift and its clinical consequence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CYP2E1 has a lower Km for ethanol than alcohol dehydrogenase, so it acts first at low ethanol levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: CYP2E1 has a higher Km for ethanol (~10 mM vs ~1 mM for ADH) and is induced by ethanol, so heavy drinking increases toxic by-product formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: CYP2E1 conjugates ethanol with sulfate to make it water soluble

choices.label: D
choices.text: CYP2E1 is a phase II enzyme that inactivates ethanol without generating any reactive species

choices.label: E
choices.text: CYP2E1 is inhibited by ethanol, decreasing its activity as alcohol intake rises

explanation.summary: CYP2E1 has a higher Km for ethanol than ADH and is induced by ethanol, so heavy drinking shifts metabolism toward the microsomal system and its toxic by-products.
explanation.whyCorrect: CYP2E1 has a much higher Km for ethanol (~10 mM) than ADH (~1 mM), so a greater fraction of ethanol is metabolized by CYP2E1 when levels are high. As a CYP450, it generates free-radical/toxic by-products, and because CYP2E1 is induced by ethanol, damage such as cirrhosis is enhanced with increased intake.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CYP2E1 has a higher, not lower, Km than ADH, which is why it dominates only at high ethanol levels.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sulfate conjugation is a phase II reaction (sulfotransferase), not the phase I oxidation performed by CYP2E1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CYP2E1 is a phase I CYP450 and does generate reactive/free-radical species that damage the liver.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP2E1 is induced by ethanol, so activity increases, not decreases, with heavier intake.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is stabilized on a benzodiazepine and a statin, both metabolized by CYP3A4. She begins drinking large amounts of fresh grapefruit juice daily. Which change in her drug metabolism is most likely, and why?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Grapefruit induces CYP3A4, lowering drug blood levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4, raising blood levels of her CYP3A4-metabolized drugs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Grapefruit has no effect because CYP3A4 metabolizes few drugs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Grapefruit activates phase II conjugation, eliminating the drugs faster

choices.label: E
choices.text: Grapefruit converts CYP3A4 into an irreversible inhibitor of hexokinase

explanation.summary: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4, so drugs cleared by CYP3A4 accumulate to higher blood levels.
explanation.whyCorrect: Citrus fruits, especially grapefruit, contain components that inhibit the enzymatic contribution of CYP3A4. If a drug is predominantly inactivated by CYP3A4, inhibiting that enzyme reduces clearance and alters (raises) the drug's blood levels compared with previous experience. CYP3A4 acts on more drugs than any other CYP450 isoform.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Grapefruit inhibits rather than induces CYP3A4; St. John's Wort is described as having an activating (inducing) effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CYP3A4 alone accounts for over one-third of hepatic CYP450 and acts on more drugs than any other isoform, so the effect is clinically significant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Grapefruit's effect is on CYP3A4 (phase I), not on accelerating phase II conjugation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Grapefruit does not turn CYP3A4 into a hexokinase inhibitor; these enzymes are unrelated.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy is brought in with scleral icterus and elevated ALT and AST after taking a large amount of acetaminophen for muscle soreness. He denies alcohol use, and his HCT and MCV are normal. Testing later reveals a polymorphic, sub-optimal variant of UDP-glucuronosyltransferase. Which mechanism best explains his jaundice and elevated liver enzymes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: His hexokinase cannot phosphorylate glucose, causing hemolysis and jaundice

choices.label: B
choices.text: His defective phase II conjugation cannot keep pace, so acetaminophen is shunted through CYP2E1 to NAPQI and bilirubin conjugation is impaired, causing hepatotoxicity and jaundice

choices.label: D
choices.text: CYP3A4 induction by acetaminophen destroys red blood cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sulfotransferase overactivity converts bilirubin into a toxic radical

explanation.summary: A weak UDP-glucuronosyltransferase variant overwhelmed by high acetaminophen impairs bilirubin conjugation and shunts drug to NAPQI, producing jaundice and liver injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: UDP-glucuronosyltransferase conjugates bilirubin for excretion; a suboptimal variant plus an abundant xenobiotic (acetaminophen) prevents adequate bilirubin conjugation, causing jaundice. With conjugation overwhelmed, more acetaminophen is oxidized by CYP2E1 to NAPQI, and elevated liver enzymes reflect hepatic damage. Alcohol can potentiate this but is not required in individuals with such variants.

whyWrongByChoice.A: His normal HCT and MCV argue against hemolysis, and hexokinase deficiency is not the described mechanism; the defect is in phase II conjugation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glutathione is protective, neutralizing NAPQI; it does not directly damage hepatocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CYP3A4 is a drug-metabolizing phase I enzyme and does not lyse red blood cells; his RBC indices are normal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sulfotransferase is a normal phase II route; it does not convert bilirubin into a toxic radical.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A patient with a large acetaminophen overdose develops rising liver enzymes because the toxic metabolite NAPQI is accumulating. Which agent is the appropriate antidote, and by what mechanism does it work?

choices.label: A
choices.text: N-acetylcysteine, because it rapidly replenishes glutathione (GSH) stores to neutralize NAPQI

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ethanol, because it inhibits CYP2E1 and stops NAPQI formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allopurinol, because it competitively conjugates NAPQI with sulfate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glucose, because hexokinase converts NAPQI to glucose 6-phosphate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Grapefruit juice, because it induces UDP-glucuronosyltransferase

explanation.summary: N-acetylcysteine replenishes GSH, which glutathione S-transferase uses to conjugate and detoxify NAPQI.
explanation.whyCorrect: NAPQI is normally conjugated with glutathione by glutathione S-transferase (forming mercapturic acid), but this system is easily saturated in overdose, letting NAPQI bind cellular cysteines and cause damage. N-acetylcysteine rapidly replenishes GSH stores to counteract NAPQI accumulation, restoring the protective conjugation pathway.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ethanol induces CYP2E1, which would increase NAPQI formation; it is not an antidote here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Allopurinol is a suicide inhibitor of xanthine oxidase and does not conjugate NAPQI with sulfate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hexokinase phosphorylates glucose, not NAPQI; this pathway is unrelated to detoxification.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Grapefruit juice inhibits CYP3A4 and does not induce UDP-glucuronosyltransferase; it would not detoxify NAPQI.

questions.topicName: Proteins, Enzymatic Machines and Introduction to Protein-based Disorders II

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man has a reactive HIV screening immunoassay. Following laboratory protocol, a confirmatory test is performed in which proteins are denatured, given a uniform negative charge, separated through a gel by size, transferred to a membrane, and then probed with labeled antibodies. Which of the following techniques is being used?

explanation.summary: Western blot separates denatured proteins by size and detects them on a membrane with antibodies.
explanation.whyCorrect: In western blotting, proteins are denatured, given a uniform negative charge by detergent, separated by size through a porous gel (largest proteins migrate least), transferred to a membrane, and probed with antibodies. HIV infection and Lyme disease still use western blotting as the confirmatory test.

whyWrongByChoice.B: ELISA uses an immobilized antigen and antibody binding in a plate; it does not separate proteins by size on a gel or membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Flow cytometry analyzes antigens on intact cells passed one at a time through a light source, not denatured proteins on a membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemoglobin electrophoresis separates proteins by charge and does not use antibodies or a membrane-transfer step.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immunohistochemistry stains antigens within a tissue biopsy examined by microscopy, not proteins run on a gel.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A 41-year-old woman with HIV requires monitoring of her absolute CD4+ T-cell count to guide therapy. The most appropriate laboratory method passes intact cells one at a time past a light source and uses labeled antibodies against surface markers to quantify how many cells express a given antigen. Which of the following techniques is this?

explanation.summary: Flow cytometry uniquely analyzes and counts antigens on intact cells one at a time.
explanation.whyCorrect: Flow cytometry uses fluid dynamics to run individual cells past a light source; light scatter separates cells by size and granularity, and antibodies against markers such as CD4 quantify the number of cells expressing that antigen within a population.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Western blot analyzes denatured proteins for size and cannot examine or count intact whole cells.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ELISA measures the abundance of a soluble antibody or antigen but does not detect antigens on intact cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An enzymatic assay quantifies enzyme activity against a standard and does not enumerate cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immunofluorescence localizes antigens within a fixed tissue biopsy by microscopy rather than counting cells in suspension.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: As part of school health protocols, a student has a reactive (positive) hepatitis B titer by ELISA and is told no follow-up is needed, while a classmate is negative and must complete a vaccination series. Which of the following best explains the significance of the student's positive titer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Active hepatitis B viral replication is present and requires treatment

choices.label: B
choices.text: A protective concentration of anti-hepatitis B antibodies is present, indicating immunity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hepatitis B viral surface protein was directly detected in the blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: The immune system has failed to respond to prior vaccination

choices.label: E
choices.text: The test measured the size of a hepatitis B protein

explanation.summary: A titer measures the relative concentration of a patient's antibody; a positive result reflects protective immunity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ELISA measures the relative quantity of anti-hepatitis B antibodies, not the virus. A reactive titer means the student has a sufficient antibody concentration for protection, so no booster is needed. The negative classmate lacks antibodies and requires vaccination.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The titer detects antibodies, not the virus, so it does not indicate active viral replication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The clinic is not measuring for the virus or its surface protein; it is measuring the patient's antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A positive titer indicates a successful, not failed, antibody response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ELISA measures antibody abundance; only western blot provides the size of a protein.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy with a family history of sickle cell disease undergoes evaluation. The disorder is caused by an alteration in the beta-globin chain of hemoglobin. Which of the following amino acid changes at position 6 of the beta subunit is responsible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A neutral valine is replaced by a negatively charged glutamate

choices.label: B
choices.text: A negatively charged glutamate is replaced by a neutral valine

choices.label: C
choices.text: A positively charged lysine is replaced by a neutral glycine

choices.label: D
choices.text: A neutral glutamine is replaced by a positively charged arginine

choices.label: E
choices.text: A hydrophobic leucine is replaced by a polar serine

explanation.summary: Sickle cell disease results from a single glutamate-to-valine substitution in beta-globin.
explanation.whyCorrect: In sickle cell disease a negatively charged glutamate is replaced by a neutral, nonpolar valine within the beta subunit at position 6. This exposes a hydrophobic residue that drives HbS polymerization when deoxygenated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the actual change; the normal glutamate is the residue lost, not gained.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lysine and glycine are not the residues involved in the sickle mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glutamine and arginine are not the residues involved in the sickle mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Leucine and serine are not the residues involved in the sickle mutation.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A hematology laboratory uses gel electrophoresis to distinguish normal adult hemoglobin (HbA) from sickle hemoglobin (HbS). Because HbA and HbS are otherwise very similar molecules, which of the following properties allows the two forms to be separated by this technique?

explanation.summary: Hemoglobin electrophoresis separates HbA from HbS on the basis of a difference in charge.
explanation.whyCorrect: Electrophoresis separates proteins by their charge at a selected pH. Because HbS lacks the negatively charged glutamate present in HbA, it carries a more positive (less negative) overall charge and migrates differently, allowing the variants to be distinguished.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The size difference between exchanging a glutamate for a valine is too small to resolve HbA from HbS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HbA and HbS have the same tetrameric quaternary structure, so shape does not separate them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although there is a slight polarity difference, hemoglobin electrophoresis does not separate proteins by polarity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemoglobin electrophoresis relies on charge migration and does not use antibodies.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A 28-year-old asymptomatic woman undergoes hemoglobin electrophoresis during pregnancy screening. Her gel shows two distinct bands: one comigrating with the HbA reference and a second, more slowly migrating HbS band. Which of the following is the most likely genotype?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Two identical HbS alleles with no HbA

explanation.summary: A heterozygous carrier shows both HbA and HbS bands on electrophoresis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A sample with both HbA and HbS bands indicates a heterozygous carrier (sickle cell trait, Beta-A/Beta-S). Carriers express both the normal and variant beta-globin and are typically asymptomatic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A normal Beta-A/Beta-A genotype would show only a single HbA band.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sickle cell anemia (Beta-S/Beta-S) produces only an HbS band, with no HbA band.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Absent beta-globin production is not consistent with two visible hemoglobin bands.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Two HbS alleles with no HbA describes sickle cell anemia, which would lack the HbA band she displays.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: On a hemoglobin electrophoresis gel, the sample is loaded near the positive pole and proteins migrate toward the negative pole. Compared with HbA, the HbS band is observed to travel farther from the positive pole. Which of the following best explains this migration pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: HbS is a larger tetramer that moves faster through the gel

choices.label: B
choices.text: HbS carries a more positive (less negative) charge than HbA

choices.label: C
choices.text: HbS has a greater net negative charge and is drawn to the positive pole

choices.label: D
choices.text: HbS is bound by antibodies that pull it toward the negative pole

choices.label: E
choices.text: HbS has already polymerized into an insoluble mass

explanation.summary: Because HbS lacks a negative glutamate, its more positive charge shifts it away from the positive pole.
explanation.whyCorrect: HbS lacks the negatively charged glutamate of HbA and therefore has a more positive overall charge. A less negative protein is drawn less strongly toward the positive pole, so HbS migrates farther from it (closer to the negative pole) than HbA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The size difference between the variants is negligible and does not explain the migration; separation here is by charge.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HbS is less negative than HbA, not more negative, so it is not pulled harder toward the positive pole.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemoglobin electrophoresis does not use antibodies to move the protein.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Migration on the gel reflects charge of the soluble protein, not polymerization state.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy with sickle cell disease presents in a painful vaso-occlusive crisis after strenuous exercise. Understanding that the sickling process is driven by a hydrophobic interaction, which of the following best describes the underlying mechanism and a key therapeutic goal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oxygenated HbS polymerizes; treatment aims to lower oxygen saturation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deoxygenated HbS exposes valine and polymerizes; treatment aims to raise oxygen saturation

choices.label: C
choices.text: A frameshift truncates beta-globin; treatment requires enzyme replacement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antibodies against RBCs cause hemolysis; treatment requires immunosuppression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Glutamate enters the hydrophobic pocket; treatment aims to reduce hydration

explanation.summary: Deoxygenation exposes the sickle valine, driving HbS polymerization; increasing oxygenation reverses it.
explanation.whyCorrect: When Hb is deoxygenated, a hydrophobic pocket forms. In HbS the abnormal valine is exposed to the polar environment and binds an adjacent deoxy-Hb tetramer, causing polymerization and rigid sickled RBCs. Because sickling is favored at low oxygen, a major intervention is to increase oxygen saturation (e.g., nasal cannula) and reduce exertion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Polymerization occurs with deoxygenated, not oxygenated, HbS; oxygenation reverses sickling, so lowering oxygen would worsen it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sickle cell disease is a point mutation, not a frameshift, and beta-globin is produced full length.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sickling is due to HbS polymerization, not antibody-mediated hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: It is the neutral valine, not glutamate, that interacts with the hydrophobic pocket in HbS.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A pathologist evaluates a breast biopsy to determine the tissue and cellular localization of a specific antigen for cancer staging. Antibodies are applied to the tissue section, and stained cells are examined under the microscope. Which of the following techniques is being used?

explanation.summary: Immunohistochemistry uses antibody-stained tissue biopsies to reveal antigen localization by microscopy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence use a patient biopsy, apply antibodies, and examine the stained sample by microscopy to determine the relative localization of an antigen in tissue. These techniques are commonly used in pathology to stage cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Flow cytometry analyzes intact cells in suspension and does not preserve tissue architecture for localization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Western blot provides antigen size from a protein mixture, not tissue localization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ELISA measures the abundance of a soluble antibody or antigen in a plate, not localization in tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An enzymatic assay measures enzyme activity and does not visualize antigen location in tissue.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: In an indirect ELISA, a laboratory-produced viral antigen is immobilized on a plastic surface and a patient's serum is added and incubated. After washing, which of the following steps allows the bound patient antibodies to be detected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Applying a labeled anti-human antibody that produces a color change with substrate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Separating the antibodies by size through a porous gel

choices.label: C
choices.text: Passing the sample one cell at a time past a laser

choices.label: D
choices.text: Staining a tissue section and viewing it by microscopy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Applying an electric field to migrate the antibodies by charge

explanation.summary: Indirect ELISA discloses bound patient antibody using a labeled anti-human antibody that generates a color signal.
explanation.whyCorrect: In an indirect ELISA, after the patient's antibodies bind the immobilized antigen, a labeled anti-human antibody is added to disclose the bound antibodies; a substrate then produces a color change, with a positive test showing color and a negative test remaining clear.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Size separation through a gel is a step in western blotting, not ELISA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Passing cells past a laser is flow cytometry, not ELISA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Staining and microscopy of a tissue section describes immunohistochemistry.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Charge-based migration in an electric field describes electrophoresis.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man has a routine liver panel that reports ALT and AST values. Unlike western blotting or flow cytometry, this measurement provides no direct visualization of the protein. Which of the following best describes how these values are obtained?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By quantifying the enzyme's activity relative to a standard

choices.label: B
choices.text: By determining the molecular weight of each enzyme

choices.label: C
choices.text: By counting the number of hepatocytes expressing the enzyme

choices.label: D
choices.text: By localizing the enzyme within a liver biopsy

choices.label: E
choices.text: By separating the enzymes on a charge-based gel

explanation.summary: Enzymatic assays report protein abundance as activity measured against a reference standard.
explanation.whyCorrect: ALT and AST in liver panels are measured by enzymatic assays tailored to detect and quantify a specific enzyme. The consumption or conversion of a substrate is measured, and protein abundance is based on the extent of enzyme activity referenced to a standard, without visualizing the protein.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Molecular weight is provided by western blotting, not by an activity-based enzymatic assay.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Counting cells that express a marker is flow cytometry, not an enzymatic assay.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Localizing an enzyme within a biopsy describes immunohistochemistry.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Charge-based gel separation describes electrophoresis, not an enzymatic activity assay.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection

questions.stem: A researcher needs to confirm both the presence and the approximate molecular weight of a specific antigen within a complex protein mixture. Which of the following techniques is uniquely suited to provide the size of the detected antigen?

explanation.summary: Western blot uniquely reports the molecular weight (size) of a detected antigen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because proteins are separated by size before antibody detection, western blot is the only listed technique that provides the molecular weight of an antigen within a mixture, in addition to confirming its presence.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ELISA reports the relative abundance of an antibody or antigen but not its size.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Flow cytometry quantifies antigens on intact cells and does not give protein molecular weight.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immunofluorescence shows antigen localization in tissue, not molecular weight.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemoglobin electrophoresis separates by charge and is not used to report antigen size in a general protein mixture.

questions.topicName: Biotechniques: Protein Detection
